title: Session 1
text:
Domain Adaptation Adjusts a model trained on a specific source distribution to perform accurately on a different but related target distribution. It minimizes the discrepancy between feature representations across domains to ensure that knowledge gained from one context generalizes to another. This process is essential when labeled data is abundant in a source domain, such as a simulation, but scarce in the target real-world application.
Transfer Learning Repurposes a model developed for one task as the starting point for a second, related task to capitalize on previously learned features. By fine-tuning weights rather than training from scratch, it significantly reduces the computational resources and data volume required for convergence. This approach connects disparate problems by identifying and leveraging shared underlying structures within the data.
Meta-learning Designs systems that improve their own learning algorithms by extracting knowledge across a wide variety of tasks to enable faster adaptation to new problems. It focuses on optimizing the learning process itself so that the model can generalize from very few examples. In the context of geometric learning, it often involves finding an inductive bias that works across different manifold structures.
Heuristic Provides a practical method for problem-solving that is not guaranteed to be optimal or perfect but is sufficient for immediate goals. These shortcuts reduce the computational complexity of searching through vast parameter spaces by prioritizing paths that are likely to succeed. They are often used in optimal transport to approximate complex distance calculations when exact solutions are too expensive.
Proxies Act as indirect measurements used to estimate a quantity or property that is difficult, expensive, or impossible to observe directly. They rely on a strong statistical correlation between the accessible variable and the target latent variable. In deep learning research, proxy tasks are frequently used to evaluate how well a model has captured the geometric properties of a dataset.
Learning Curves Track how a model’s performance metrics, such as loss or accuracy, evolve as a function of training time or the amount of data processed. These visualizations reveal whether a model is bottlenecked by its architecture or if it would benefit from additional optimization. They serve as a primary diagnostic tool for identifying overfitting or underfitting during the training cycle.
Optimal Parameters Represent the specific internal weights and biases that minimize a chosen cost function for a given task and dataset. These values are the result of the optimization process and define the learned mapping between inputs and outputs. Finding these parameters is the central goal of training, as they encapsulate the model’s “understanding” of the underlying data geometry.
Co-occurrence Measures the frequency with which two distinct elements or labels appear together within the same context or sample. It provides a basis for understanding the relational structure of a dataset by highlighting statistical dependencies between different features. Analyzing these patterns is vital for building embeddings that preserve the semantic or topological relationships of the input space.
Transfer Performance Quantifies the immediate accuracy of a model on a new dataset to determine the similarity between the original training data and the new environment. High performance without additional training suggests that the underlying distributions share significant geometric or statistical features. It serves as a functional metric for measuring the distance between different data domains.
Weight Similarity Calculates the degree of alignment between the optimal parameters of two different models to determine if their respective tasks require similar internal representations. If the learned weights are nearly identical, the tasks can be treated as proxies for one another, indicating a deep structural overlap in the problems being solved. This metric offers a direct way to compare tasks by looking at the specific features the models prioritize.
Learning Curve Slopes Compare the rate of improvement over time between two different models to determine if they are discovering patterns at a similar pace. When these rates are nearly identical, it suggests that the tasks share a common underlying complexity or structural logic. This metric provides a dynamic view of task similarity that static snapshots of accuracy might miss.
Feature Distributions Measure the statistical properties of the raw input data, such as pixel intensity or texture patterns, to characterize the visual or mathematical domain. If these distributions align across two datasets, they can be treated as proxies for the same environment, ensuring that a model’s low-level feature detectors remain valid. This alignment is a prerequisite for successful domain adaptation and stable geometric representations.
Label Co-occurrence Identifies how frequently specific categories or tags appear together within a dataset to map out semantic dependencies. By establishing that one label consistently predicts the presence of another, researchers can use broader categories as functional proxies for more granular ones. This relationship helps models navigate high-dimensional label spaces by exploiting the natural hierarchy of the data.
Probe Model Evaluates the specific characteristics or quality of a dataset by using a simplified diagnostic architecture rather than a production-level system. It acts as a specialized tool to “interrogate” what information is accessible within a set of feature representations. Probing is essential for understanding whether a specific geometric property, like curvature or connectivity, has been successfully captured during training.
Training to Completion Executes an optimization algorithm until the model reaches a state where performance metrics stabilize and no further improvement is observed. This ensures that the model has fully converged to its optimal parameters and that the resulting data is a true reflection of the model’s capacity. It is a critical baseline for comparing different architectures, as it removes the variable of insufficient training time.
Principled Notion Refers to a methodology or theoretical framework derived from rigorous mathematical proofs rather than empirical trial-and-error. These approaches provide guarantees regarding stability, convergence, and uniqueness, which are vital for reliable performance in sensitive applications. In optimal transport, a principled approach ensures that the calculated distances satisfy all the requirements of a true metric.
Hierarchical OT Distance Computes the difference between complex objects by nesting optimal transport calculations, where the cost of moving individual elements is itself determined by a lower-level transport plan. This multi-layered approach allows for the comparison of structured data, such as a collection of point clouds or a sequence of images. It preserves the local geometry of the components while simultaneously accounting for the global arrangement of the whole.
Optimal Transport Calculates the minimum total “work” required to transform one probability distribution into another according to a specific cost function. It provides a geometrically sensitive way to compare datasets by looking at how mass must be redistributed across a space. This theory is the foundational engine for aligning distributions in domain adaptation and measuring distances on manifold structures.
Feature-Label Pairs Represent the fundamental unit of supervised learning, consisting of an input data point and its associated ground-truth annotation. This pairing allows the model to learn the specific mapping or transformation required to predict outcomes from raw data. The quality and diversity of these pairs directly dictate the model’s ability to generalize to unseen examples within the same geometric space.
Geometrically-meaningful Describes a distance measure or transformation that respects the intrinsic shape, curvature, and spatial relationships of the data points. Instead of treating dimensions as independent, it accounts for the actual structure of the underlying manifold. This ensures that the model’s operations align with the physical or logical reality of the problem, leading to more robust and interpretable results.
Distributions Describe the frequency and spread of all possible values within a dataset, providing a complete statistical profile of a variable. They allow researchers to visualize the underlying structure of data, such as where most points cluster and how far the outliers extend. In optimal transport, the goal is often to find the most efficient way to map one distribution onto another while preserving these statistical properties.
Discrepancy Distance Quantifies the functional difference between two data sources by measuring the maximum change in a predictor’s performance when moving from one to the other. It identifies how sensitive a specific set of models is to changes in the data environment, highlighting where domain shifts might cause failure. This metric is a central tool in domain adaptation for bounding the error of a model transferred to a new target domain.
Loss Function Calculates a numerical penalty representing the divergence between a model’s prediction and the true target value. This value serves as a feedback signal that guides the optimization process, directing the model to adjust its weights to minimize error. Choosing an appropriate loss function, such as a Wasserstein-based loss, can fundamentally change how a model perceives the geometry of its mistakes.
Hypothesis Class Defines the entire collection of possible functions or models that an algorithm is permitted to consider during the learning process. It constrains the search space, ensuring the model remains within a specific family of shapes or complexities, such as linear functions or deep neural networks. The size and flexibility of this class determine the trade-off between the model’s ability to fit data and its tendency to overfit.
Supremum Identifies the least upper bound of a set or function, representing the smallest value that is greater than or equal to all elements in that set. It is used to define the “worst-case” scenario or maximum possible deviation within a mathematical space, even if that exact value is never perfectly reached. In geometric deep learning, the supremum is essential for defining robust distances that account for the maximum possible error across a domain.
Generalization Bounds Provide mathematical guarantees on a model’s future performance by establishing a limit on the potential error it will encounter on unseen data. These bounds are calculated using the model’s training error combined with a measure of the model’s complexity and the size of the dataset. They offer a principled way to estimate reliability and ensure that the model has truly learned a general rule rather than memorizing noise.
VC-dimension Measures the capacity or flexibility of a hypothesis class by determining the maximum number of data points it can perfectly classify regardless of their labels. A higher value indicates a more powerful model capable of learning intricate patterns, but it also increases the risk of finding false patterns in the training data. This metric is fundamental to statistical learning theory for predicting when a model is likely to generalize well.
Fisher Information Metric Quantifies the amount of information that an observable random variable carries about an unknown parameter of its underlying distribution. It defines a local Riemannian metric on the space of probability distributions, turning statistical models into geometric manifolds. This metric is the cornerstone of natural gradient descent, allowing optimization to follow the steepest path through the actual “shape” of the information.
Information Geometry Investigates probability theory and statistics by treating families of probability distributions as geometric manifolds. It applies concepts like curvature, distance, and geodesics to understand how distributions relate to one another in a structured space. This field provides the mathematical bridge that allows geometric deep learning to treat statistical models as physical shapes that can be navigated and optimized.
Parameterized Probability Distributions Represent families of statistical curves whose specific shapes are controlled by a fixed set of adjustable numbers, such as a mean or standard deviation. By tuning these parameters, a model can approximate the complex distributions found in real-world data within a known mathematical framework. These distributions are the primary objects of study in computational optimal transport, where the goal is to move between them with minimal cost.
Density Sensitivity Measures how much the local probability mass at specific points in the data space shifts in response to changes in the model’s internal parameters. It tracks whether small adjustments to the model cause large reorganizations of where the “weight” of the distribution is placed. This sensitivity is a key factor in understanding the stability of generative models and the smoothness of the underlying data manifold.
Capacity Defines the maximum complexity and volume of patterns that a neural network architecture is mathematically capable of encoding. It is determined by the total number of trainable parameters and the specific constraints of the model’s wiring. A model with insufficient capacity will fail to capture the nuances of a dataset, while excessive capacity without proper regularization often leads to memorization rather than learning.
Vector Representations Transform complex concepts, such as images or text, into a structured list of numerical coordinates within a high-dimensional space. By mapping data into this format, computers can use linear algebra to calculate similarities and perform geometric operations on abstract ideas. These vectors serve as the primary language of deep learning, allowing different types of information to be compared in a unified mathematical framework.
Taxonomic Similarities Define relationships between data points or labels based on organized, hierarchical classification systems. It measures how closely two items are related within a tree of categories, such as identifying that a “Sedan” is closer to a “Truck” than to a “Bicycle.” This structured approach helps models leverage prior knowledge about the world to make more logical predictions in sparse data environments.
Agnosticism Describes the capability of a mathematical method or algorithm to function effectively without requiring prior knowledge of specific task details or data labels. It ensures that the underlying logic remains valid regardless of the number of categories or the specific nature of the input features. In computational optimal transport, agnostic methods are highly valued because they can be applied to diverse domains without manual reconfiguration.
Semantics Focuses on the underlying meaning and logical relationships of data labels rather than their literal string names. It allows a system to recognize that different terms, such as “Canine” and “Dog,” represent the same concept within the data geometry. Capturing these relationships is essential for building models that understand the world conceptually, preventing them from treating closely related categories as entirely distinct entities.
Information Theoretic Refers to concepts derived from the mathematical study of how information is quantified, compressed, and transmitted. It uses measures like entropy and mutual information to evaluate how much “knowledge” a model has successfully extracted from its training source. This perspective is vital for determining the efficiency of learning algorithms and the theoretical limits of data representation.
Kolmogorov Structure Function Measures the complexity of a dataset by identifying the shortest possible algorithm or description required to recreate it. It distinguishes between the meaningful, structured part of the data and the random, incompressible noise. This concept provides a fundamental way to define what it means for a model to “understand” data by finding the most compact representation of its core logic.
Reachability Quantifies the ease with which a model optimized for one task can be modified or fine-tuned to master a second, different task. It assesses the geometric distance between the optimal parameter states for the two problems within the model’s weight space. High reachability indicates that the tasks are structurally compatible, making them ideal candidates for transfer learning or multi-task optimization.
Distributional Distance Calculates the overall difference between two datasets by comparing their statistical shapes and mass allocations rather than matching individual data points. This global view accounts for the spread, variance, and clusters within the data, providing a more robust metric than point-to-point comparisons. It is the core concept behind Wasserstein distances and other optimal transport tools used to align disparate data sources.
Closed-form Provides a mathematical solution that can be expressed using a finite set of standard operations, such as addition, multiplication, or functions like square roots and logarithms. It allows for an exact result to be calculated instantly without the need for iterative approximation or “trial and error” loops. These expressions are highly prized in computational optimal transport because they offer maximum efficiency and numerical stability compared to algorithmic solvers.
Wasserstein Distance Measures the total “effort” required to transform one probability distribution into another by accounting for both the amount of mass moved and the distance it must travel. It provides a geometrically sensitive way to compare data by looking at how mass must be redistributed across a space. Often called the Earth Mover’s Distance, it is the primary building block for optimal transport-based comparisons and remains effective even when two distributions do not overlap.

When these rates are nearly identical, it suggests that the tasks share a common underlying complexity or structural logic. This metric provides a dynamic view of task similarity that static snapshots of accuracy might miss.
Feature Distributions Measure the statistical properties of the raw input data, such as pixel intensity or texture patterns, to characterize the visual or mathematical domain. If these distributions align across two datasets, they can be treated as proxies for the same environment, ensuring that a model’s low-level feature detectors remain valid. This alignment is a prerequisite for successful domain adaptation and stable geometric representations.
Label Co-occurrence Identifies how frequently specific categories or tags appear together within a dataset to map out semantic dependencies. By establishing that one label consistently predicts the presence of another, researchers can use broader categories as functional proxies for more granular ones. This relationship helps models navigate high-dimensional label spaces by exploiting the natural hierarchy of the data.
Probe Model Evaluates the specific characteristics or quality of a dataset by using a simplified diagnostic architecture rather than a production-level system. It acts as a specialized tool to “interrogate” what information is accessible within a set of feature representations. Probing is essential for understanding whether a specific geometric property, like curvature or connectivity, has been successfully captured during training.
Training to Completion Executes an optimization algorithm until the model reaches a state where performance metrics stabilize and no further improvement is observed. This ensures that the model has fully converged to its optimal parameters and that the resulting data is a true reflection of the model’s capacity. It is a critical baseline for comparing different architectures, as it removes the variable of insufficient training time.
Principled Notion Refers to a methodology or theoretical framework derived from rigorous mathematical proofs rather than empirical trial-and-error. These approaches provide guarantees regarding stability, convergence, and uniqueness, which are vital for reliable performance in sensitive applications. In optimal transport, a principled approach ensures that the calculated distances satisfy all the requirements of a true metric.
Hierarchical OT Distance Computes the difference between complex objects by nesting optimal transport calculations, where the cost of moving individual elements is itself determined by a lower-level transport plan. This multi-layered approach allows for the comparison of structured data, such as a collection of point clouds or a sequence of images. It preserves the local geometry of the components while simultaneously accounting for the global arrangement of the whole.
Optimal Transport Calculates the minimum total “work” required to transform one probability distribution into another according to a specific cost function. It provides a geometrically sensitive way to compare datasets by looking at how mass must be redistributed across a space. This theory is the foundational engine for aligning distributions in domain adaptation and measuring distances on manifold structures.
Feature-Label Pairs Represent the fundamental unit of supervised learning, consisting of an input data point and its associated ground-truth annotation. This pairing allows the model to learn the specific mapping or transformation required to predict outcomes from raw data. The quality and diversity of these pairs directly dictate the model’s ability to generalize to unseen examples within the same geometric space.
Geometrically-meaningful Describes a distance measure or transformation that respects the intrinsic shape, curvature, and spatial relationships of the data points. Instead of treating dimensions as independent, it accounts for the actual structure of the underlying manifold. This ensures that the model’s operations align with the physical or logical reality of the problem, leading to more robust and interpretable results.
Distributions Describe the frequency and spread of all possible values within a dataset, providing a complete statistical profile of a variable. They allow researchers to visualize the underlying structure of data, such as where most points cluster and how far the outliers extend. In optimal transport, the goal is often to find the most efficient way to map one distribution onto another while preserving these statistical properties.
Discrepancy Distance Quantifies the functional difference between two data sources by measuring the maximum change in a predictor’s performance when moving from one to the other. It identifies how sensitive a specific set of models is to changes in the data environment, highlighting where domain shifts might cause failure. This metric is a central tool in domain adaptation for bounding the error of a model transferred to a new target domain.
Loss Function Calculates a numerical penalty representing the divergence between a model’s prediction and the true target value. This value serves as a feedback signal that guides the optimization process, directing the model to adjust its weights to minimize error. Choosing an appropriate loss function, such as a Wasserstein-based loss, can fundamentally change how a model perceives the geometry of its mistakes.
Hypothesis Class Defines the entire collection of possible functions or models that an algorithm is permitted to consider during the learning process. It constrains the search space, ensuring the model remains within a specific family of shapes or complexities, such as linear functions or deep neural networks. The size and flexibility of this class determine the trade-off between the model’s ability to fit data and its tendency to overfit.
Supremum Identifies the least upper bound of a set or function, representing the smallest value that is greater than or equal to all elements in that set. It is used to define the “worst-case” scenario or maximum possible deviation within a mathematical space, even if that exact value is never perfectly reached. In geometric deep learning, the supremum is essential for defining robust distances that account for the maximum possible error across a domain.
Generalization Bounds Provide mathematical guarantees on a model’s future performance by establishing a limit on the potential error it will encounter on unseen data. These bounds are calculated using the model’s training error combined with a measure of the model’s complexity and the size of the dataset. They offer a principled way to estimate reliability and ensure that the model has truly learned a general rule rather than memorizing noise.
VC-dimension Measures the capacity or flexibility of a hypothesis class by determining the maximum number of data points it can perfectly classify regardless of their labels. A higher value indicates a more powerful model capable of learning intricate patterns, but it also increases the risk of finding false patterns in the training data. This metric is fundamental to statistical learning theory for predicting when a model is likely to generalize well.
Fisher Information Metric Quantifies the amount of information that an observable random variable carries about an unknown parameter of its underlying distribution. It defines a local Riemannian metric on the space of probability distributions, turning statistical models into geometric manifolds. This metric is the cornerstone of natural gradient descent, allowing optimization to follow the steepest path through the actual “shape” of the information.
Information Geometry Investigates probability theory and statistics by treating families of probability distributions as geometric manifolds. It applies concepts like curvature, distance, and geodesics to understand how distributions relate to one another in a structured space. This field provides the mathematical bridge that allows geometric deep learning to treat statistical models as physical shapes that can be navigated and optimized.
Parameterized Probability Distributions Represent families of statistical curves whose specific shapes are controlled by a fixed set of adjustable numbers, such as a mean or standard deviation. By tuning these parameters, a model can approximate the complex distributions found in real-world data within a known mathematical framework. These distributions are the primary objects of study in computational optimal transport, where the goal is to move between them with minimal cost.
Density Sensitivity Measures how much the local probability mass at specific points in the data space shifts in response to changes in the model’s internal parameters. It tracks whether small adjustments to the model cause large reorganizations of where the “weight” of the distribution is placed. This sensitivity is a key factor in understanding the stability of generative models and the smoothness of the underlying data manifold.
Capacity Defines the maximum complexity and volume of patterns that a neural network architecture is mathematically capable of encoding. It is determined by the total number of trainable parameters and the specific constraints of the model’s wiring. A model with insufficient capacity will fail to capture the nuances of a dataset, while excessive capacity without proper regularization often leads to memorization rather than learning.
Vector Representations Transform complex concepts, such as images or text, into a structured list of numerical coordinates within a high-dimensional space. By mapping data into this format, computers can use linear algebra to calculate similarities and perform geometric operations on abstract ideas. These vectors serve as the primary language of deep learning, allowing different types of information to be compared in a unified mathematical framework.
Taxonomic Similarities Define relationships between data points or labels based on organized, hierarchical classification systems. It measures how closely two items are related within a tree of categories, such as identifying that a “Sedan” is closer to a “Truck” than to a “Bicycle.” This structured approach helps models leverage prior knowledge about the world to make more logical predictions in sparse data environments.
Agnosticism Describes the capability of a mathematical method or algorithm to function effectively without requiring prior knowledge of specific task details or data labels. It ensures that the underlying logic remains valid regardless of the number of categories or the specific nature of the input features. In computational optimal transport, agnostic methods are highly valued because they can be applied to diverse domains without manual reconfiguration.
Semantics Focuses on the underlying meaning and logical relationships of data labels rather than their literal string names. It allows a system to recognize that different terms, such as “Canine” and “Dog,” represent the same concept within the data geometry. Capturing these relationships is essential for building models that understand the world conceptually, preventing them from treating closely related categories as entirely distinct entities.
Information Theoretic Refers to concepts derived from the mathematical study of how information is quantified, compressed, and transmitted. It uses measures like entropy and mutual information to evaluate how much “knowledge” a model has successfully extracted from its training source. This perspective is vital for determining the efficiency of learning algorithms and the theoretical limits of data representation.
Kolmogorov Structure Function Measures the complexity of a dataset by identifying the shortest possible algorithm or description required to recreate it. It distinguishes between the meaningful, structured part of the data and the random, incompressible noise. This concept provides a fundamental way to define what it means for a model to “understand” data by finding the most compact representation of its core logic.
Reachability Quantifies the ease with which a model optimized for one task can be modified or fine-tuned to master a second, different task. It assesses the geometric distance between the optimal parameter states for the two problems within the model’s weight space. High reachability indicates that the tasks are structurally compatible, making them ideal candidates for transfer learning or multi-task optimization.
Distributional Distance Calculates the overall difference between two datasets by comparing their statistical shapes and mass allocations rather than matching individual data points. This global view accounts for the spread, variance, and clusters within the data, providing a more robust metric than point-to-point comparisons. It is the core concept behind Wasserstein distances and other optimal transport tools used to align disparate data sources.
Closed-form Provides a mathematical solution that can be expressed using a finite set of standard operations, such as addition, multiplication, or functions like square roots and logarithms. It allows for an exact result to be calculated instantly without the need for iterative approximation or “trial and error” loops. These expressions are highly prized in computational optimal transport because they offer maximum efficiency and numerical stability compared to algorithmic solvers.
Wasserstein Distance Measures the total “effort” required to transform one probability distribution into another by accounting for both the amount of mass moved and the distance it must travel. It provides a geometrically sensitive way to compare data by looking at how mass must be redistributed across a space. Often called the Earth Mover’s Distance, it is the primary building block for optimal transport-based comparisons and remains effective even when two distributions do not overlap.

Domain Adaptation Adjusts a model trained on a specific source distribution to perform accurately on a different but related target distribution. It minimizes the discrepancy between feature representations across domains to ensure that knowledge gained from one context generalizes to another. This process is essential when labeled data is abundant in a source domain, such as a simulation, but scarce in the target real-world application.
Transfer Learning Repurposes a model developed for one task as the starting point for a second, related task to capitalize on previously learned features. By fine-tuning weights rather than training from scratch, it significantly reduces the computational resources and data volume required for convergence. This approach connects disparate problems by identifying and leveraging shared underlying structures within the data.
Meta-learning Designs systems that improve their own learning algorithms by extracting knowledge across a wide variety of tasks to enable faster adaptation to new problems. It focuses on optimizing the learning process itself so that the model can generalize from very few examples. In the context of geometric learning, it often involves finding an inductive bias that works across different manifold structures.

These shortcuts reduce the computational complexity of searching through vast parameter spaces by prioritizing paths that are likely to succeed. They are often used in optimal transport to approximate complex distance calculations when exact solutions are too expensive.

They rely on a strong statistical correlation between the accessible variable and the target latent variable. title: Shreesh Bhattarai
text:
Structurally Identical: A state where two graphs possess the same topology and connectivity, despite having different node orderings or feature assignments. Formally known as isomorphism, this condition implies that the graphs are functionally equivalent and should yield the same output from a valid geometric model. Ensuring a model can recognize structural identity is a benchmark for its ability to generalize across different graph representations.
Remapping (Re-indexing): The technical process of updating the pointers or integer identifiers within a graph’s adjacency structure to reflect changes in the input ordering. This operation is necessary when graph datasets are pre-processed or shuffled, ensuring that the edge_index correctly identifies the node connections after the data has been reorganized. Efficient remapping is essential for maintaining data integrity during batching and model training.
Node IDs: The unique integer identifiers (typically \(0\) to \(N-1\)) assigned to each vertex in a graph to designate its position within the adjacency and feature matrices. These IDs provide the framework for defining edges, as they act as the coordinate system for the edge_index tensor. While the model should be permutation-invariant/equivariant to these IDs, they are the strictly required “address” for all computational operations.
Permutation Vector: A mathematical representation of a specific reordering, where the value at each position indicates the new target location for the element originally at that index. This vector is used to transform input tensors consistently, ensuring that if node \(i\) moves to position \(j\), its associated features and edge relationships are tracked accordingly. It is a vital tool for implementing and verifying permutation-equivariance in neural network architectures.
Spectral Convolution: A signal processing technique that performs convolutions on graphs by projecting signals into the frequency domain using the graph Laplacian’s eigenvectors. It applies filters to the graph’s “vibrations,” effectively identifying global patterns by scaling specific frequency components. This approach provides a mathematically rigorous way to define convolution, contrasting with the spatial methods that aggregate local neighborhood features.
Computational Complexity (\(O(N^3)\) vs \(O(E)\)): A notation used to quantify how an algorithm’s execution time scales as the input size increases, with \(O(N^3)\) indicating cubic growth and \(O(E)\) indicating linear growth relative to the number of edges. Understanding this difference is critical for selecting models that are scalable, as \(O(N^3)\) methods become computationally prohibitive for large networks. \(O(E)\) implementations, typical in modern MPNNs, allow for efficient processing of massive datasets by only visiting existing connections.
Globally Supported: A filtering characteristic where each node receives information from every other node in the graph, regardless of the physical distance between them. This occurs when filters are defined on the full spectrum of the Laplacian, allowing the model to capture large-scale, long-range dependencies in a single layer. While powerful for understanding overall structure, these filters can be computationally intensive and prone to oversmoothing.
Locally Supported: A filtering characteristic where the transformation of a node’s features depends only on the features of its immediate neighbors within a fixed \(k\)-hop distance. This locality allows for efficient computation, as operations are restricted to the sparse connectivity defined by the edge_index. It is the operational basis for most standard Message Passing Neural Networks, favoring inductive learning and scalability over capturing absolute global graph structure.
Chebyshev Polynomials: A recursive sequence of orthogonal polynomials used to approximate complex spectral filters without requiring the expensive eigendecomposition of the graph Laplacian. They allow for the efficient calculation of localized graph convolutions by representing the filter as a truncated expansion of the Laplacian matrix. This approximation is the mathematical foundation of ChebNet, enabling spectral-like filtering with the computational efficiency of spatial methods.
\(\lambda_{max}\): The largest eigenvalue of the graph Laplacian, which defines the upper bound of the graph’s frequency spectrum and the fastest possible rate of signal variation across edges. It serves as a critical normalization factor in spectral filters to ensure that numerical operations remain within a stable range, typically \([-1, 1]\). In geometric deep learning, scaling by this value prevents the exploding gradients that occur when repeatedly applying the Laplacian operator.
Numerical Stability: The property of an algorithm to remain accurate and reliable even when subjected to the inherent precision limits of digital computation. It ensures that small errors in input or intermediate steps do not amplify into significant inaccuracies that could crash a model or lead to divergent gradients. Achieving this often involves techniques like renormalization or adding a small epsilon value to denominators to prevent division by zero.
Non-Associative: A mathematical property of certain operations where the grouping of terms changes the final output, even if the sequence remains the same. While standard addition is associative, many floating-point operations in parallel computing can behave non-associatively due to the way small decimals are rounded during intermediate steps. This can lead to slight discrepancies in model outputs when the same code is run on different hardware or with different batch sizes.
Floating-Point: A technical standard for representing real numbers in a way that balances range and precision using a fixed number of bits to store a significand and an exponent. Because it approximates continuous numbers using discrete binary values, it is the primary source of rounding behavior in scientific computing. Understanding this representation is essential for diagnosing why two theoretically identical mathematical paths might produce slightly different numerical results on a GPU.
Precision Error: The cumulative discrepancy between a true mathematical value and its digital approximation caused by the finite memory of a computer. These errors are most prominent when performing millions of successive operations, such as during the backpropagation of a deep neural network, where tiny rounding differences can aggregate. In GDL, these errors are why structural identity tests must often rely on thresholds rather than exact bitwise equality.
torch.allclose: A PyTorch utility function that determines if two tensors are element-wise equal within a specified tolerance range. It accounts for the unavoidable noise introduced by floating-point arithmetic by checking if the absolute difference between values is smaller than a combination of a relative and absolute epsilon. This is the standard tool for “smoke testing” GDL models to ensure that re-indexing or permutations have not fundamentally altered the underlying data.
WL-Indistinguishable: A condition where two non-isomorphic graphs produce the same canonical hash during a Weisfeiler-Lehman test, causing the algorithm to falsely identify them as identical. This occurs when the local neighborhood structures are so symmetrical that simple color-refinement cannot break the tie, such as in certain regular graphs. It represents the fundamental theoretical limit of standard Message Passing Neural Networks, which cannot distinguish between such structures.
Multiset: A generalization of the set concept where elements are allowed to appear more than once, and their specific frequencies are preserved. In the context of the WL test or GNN aggregation, a multiset of neighbor features ensures that the model can distinguish between a node connected to two “Oxygen” atoms versus a node connected to only one. Formally, a GNN must use an injective function over these multisets to reach the maximum expressive power of the WL-test.
Hashing: A process that maps an arbitrary input, such as a multiset of neighbor labels, to a unique fixed-size identifier or “color.” In graph learning, hashing is used to iteratively refine node representations by creating a distinct signature for every unique combination of local features and topology. Effective hashing ensures that nodes with different structural roles are assigned different states, allowing the model to distinguish between complex chemical environments.
Expressive Power: A theoretical measure of the range of functions or structural patterns a neural network is capable of representing and distinguishing. In the context of graph learning, it is often quantified by comparing a model’s ability to identify non-isomorphic graphs against the Weisfeiler-Lehman test. Higher expressive power allows a model to capture more complex chemical or topological nuances, such as cycles or long-range symmetries, that simpler architectures might ignore.
Methodological Error: A flaw in the design, execution, or evaluation of a technical experiment that compromises the validity and reproducibility of its results. In geometric deep learning, common examples include data leakage between training and test sets or using inconsistent metrics to compare different architectures. Identifying and correcting these errors is vital for ensuring that performance gains are genuine and not artifacts of a biased setup.
Z-score Normalization: A data preprocessing technique that rescales feature values to have a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one. This transformation ensures that disparate features, such as atomic mass and electronegativity, are on a comparable numerical scale, preventing any single variable from dominating the model’s gradient updates. It is a critical step for improving the training stability and convergence speed of neural networks.
Cost Matrix: A matrix that records the “distance” or penalty for moving a unit of mass from a specific source location to a specific target location. It defines the geometry of the underlying space, such as Euclidean distance or a path-based metric on a graph, which dictates the difficulty of various transport paths. This matrix is the fundamental input for any optimal transport problem, as it determines which transformations are considered the most efficient.
Kantorovich Dual: An alternative formulation of the optimal transport problem that focuses on finding potential functions for the source and target distributions rather than the explicit transport plan. It transforms a constrained minimization over many variables into a simpler maximization problem, which is often more computationally tractable for high-dimensional data. This dual perspective is the mathematical foundation for many modern Wasserstein-based loss functions in deep learning.
Sinkhorn Divergence: A regularized version of the Wasserstein distance that adds an entropy term to the optimization problem, allowing it to be solved much faster using the Sinkhorn-Knopp algorithm. This adjustment turns a hard linear programming problem into a smooth, differentiable calculation that can be easily integrated into neural network training loops. It provides a practical compromise between the mathematical purity of optimal transport and the performance requirements of large-scale AI.
Displacement Interpolation: A method of transitioning between two probability distributions by moving mass along the shortest possible paths, known as geodesics, rather than simply fading pixels or values in place. Unlike linear interpolation, which creates a blurry “ghosting” effect, this technique preserves the geometric structure and sharpness of the data as it evolves. It is the gold standard for “morphing” between shapes, images, or molecular conformations in a physically meaningful way.
Push-Forward Measure: The resulting distribution created when a set of points or a mass is transformed by a specific function or mapping. It describes how the “weight” of the original data is redistributed across a new space after undergoing a movement like transport or a neural network layer. Tracking this measure is essential for understanding how generative models reshape simple noise into complex data like realistic images or stable molecules.
Injective Aggregation: A type of aggregation function, such as a sum over a multiset, that produces a unique output for every unique combination of inputs. In Message Passing Neural Networks, using an injective operator is the key to ensuring the model has the maximum possible expressive power, allowing it to distinguish between different neighborhood structures. If the aggregation is not injective, the model may “collapse” different graph topologies into the same representation, losing critical structural information.
Edge Index: A coordinate-style tensor that explicitly lists the pairs of source and target nodes that form the connections within a graph. It is the primary way that libraries like PyTorch Geometric store topology, typically as a 2-by-E matrix of integers. This representation is highly efficient for sparse graphs and allows the model to quickly identify which node features should be aggregated during the message-passing step.
Global Pooling: An operation that collapses all node-level representations in a graph into a single vector that describes the entire graph as a whole. Common methods include taking the global sum or mean of all node features after several layers of message passing have occurred. This step is necessary for graph-level tasks, such as classifying a molecule’s toxicity or a social network’s community type.
Attention Mechanism (Graph Attention): A specialized aggregation technique that assigns different “weights” or importance levels to different neighbors during the message-passing process. Instead of treating all neighbors equally, the model learns which connections are most relevant for the current task, such as identifying the specific bond that determines a molecule’s reactivity. This allows the model to focus on critical structural features while filtering out noise from less important parts of the graph.
Oversmoothing: A phenomenon in deep GNNs where node representations become increasingly similar to one another after too many layers of message passing. As information is averaged across larger and larger neighborhoods, the distinct features of individual nodes are washed out, eventually leading to a loss of predictive power. Preventing this is a major challenge in GDL, often requiring specialized architectures like residual connections or jumping knowledge networks.
Wasserstein Geodesic: The shortest path between two probability distributions within the space of distributions, where “distance” is defined by the Wasserstein metric. It represents the most efficient continuous transformation from one shape to another, ensuring that every intermediate step is also a valid distribution. In generative modeling, following this path ensures that the transition between two data points is smooth and physically plausible.
Entropy Regularization: A technique that adds a small “blurring” or noise term to the optimal transport objective to make the problem easier and faster to solve. It smooths out the transport plan, allowing the use of efficient iterative algorithms like Sinkhorn’s instead of heavy linear programming. While it introduces a slight approximation error, it is essential for making optimal transport differentiable and scalable for deep learning.
Plan Matrix (\(\pi\)): A grid where the value at row \(i\) and column \(j\) indicates exactly how much mass is being moved from source location \(i\) to target location \(j\). It acts as the “map” for the optimal transport problem, satisfying the constraint that all mass from the source must be accounted for and the target must be completely filled. This matrix reveals the specific correspondences and alignments found between two different datasets.
Softmax Aggregation: A differentiable method of combining neighbor information where a “temperature” parameter controls whether the model focuses on the strongest signal or averages all signals equally. It allows the network to learn how to weight neighbors dynamically, providing a middle ground between a simple mean and a hard maximum. This flexibility is particularly useful when some connections in a graph are significantly more informative than others.
Log-Sum-Exp Trick: A numerical technique used to compute the logarithm of the sum of exponentials in a way that prevents computer memory from “overflowing” with massive numbers. By shifting the values by their maximum before exponentiating, the algorithm maintains high precision and stability during calculations like Softmax or entropy-regularized transport. This is a mandatory implementation detail for ensuring that deep learning models don’t crash during training.
Virtual Node: An artificial vertex added to a graph that is connected to every other node, acting as a global communication hub. It allows information to travel across the entire graph in just two steps, bypassing the “limited reach” problem of standard message passing. This is a common architectural trick used to capture long-range dependencies in large molecules or social networks without requiring dozens of layers.
Readout Function: The final layer in a graph neural network that aggregates all refined node features into a single global vector for the entire graph. It must be permutation-invariant to ensure that the final graph-level prediction is independent of how the nodes were originally indexed. This function is what translates the “local” knowledge gained through message passing into a “global” property like a molecule’s toxicity.
Barycenter: The “average” distribution of a collection of multiple input distributions, calculated by finding the one shape that minimizes the total Wasserstein distance to all others. It acts as a geometric mean that preserves the structural features of the group rather than just averaging their pixel values or coordinates. This concept is used to synthesize a single representative template from a diverse set of examples, such as an “average” brain scan or a “consensus” molecular structure.
Cayley Polynomials: A sophisticated sequence of functions used in spectral GDL to define filters that can detect both low-frequency smoothness and high-frequency edges with greater flexibility than standard polynomials. They utilize complex numbers and a specialized scaling factor to allow for “zooming in” on specific parts of the graph’s spectrum, providing higher resolution in areas where structural details are dense. These filters are particularly useful for graphs with irregular topologies where traditional spectral methods struggle to maintain local focus.
Jumping Knowledge (JK) Networks: An architectural design that connects the intermediate features from every layer of a GNN directly to the final output layer, rather than only using the last layer’s result. This allows the model to adaptively choose the best “neighborhood range” for each node, using local info for some and global info for others depending on the graph’s local density. It effectively solves the problem of information getting lost or becoming too blurry as it passes through many successive rounds of message passing.
Edge Convolution (EdgeConv): A dynamic operation that builds a local neighborhood for each point by finding its nearest neighbors in the feature space, even if they aren’t connected in the original input. It recalculates these connections at every layer of the neural network, allowing the graph’s “shape” to evolve as the model learns which points are most similar to one another. This technique is the engine behind models like DGCNN, which are highly effective at understanding 3D point clouds and complex geometric manifolds.

title: Session 3
text:
Zero Mass: A condition where a specific point or outcome within a distribution has a probability of exactly zero, meaning no “weight” is assigned to that location. It defines the regions where a probability measure is inactive or empty, which is critical for determining the boundaries of a distribution. Identifying these regions helps establish the support of a measure and dictates where transport plans cannot send or receive mass.
Mass: The total amount of probability assigned to a specific point or region, representing the “weight” that must be accounted for during transport. In discrete settings, it refers to the value of the probability mass function at a given coordinate. It serves as the fundamental substance that is shifted or redistributed when comparing two different datasets or distributions.
Support Points: The specific coordinates in a space where a probability measure is non-zero, representing the actual locations of data points. These points define the physical “footprint” of a distribution and serve as the source and destination indices for any transport calculation. Knowing the support is essential because optimal transport only concerns itself with moving mass between these defined locations.
Cost Matrix: A structured array that stores the individual penalties or distances associated with moving mass from every source location to every destination location. Each entry \(C_{ij}\) quantifies the effort required to transport one unit of mass from point \(i\) to point \(j\). It acts as the ground metric that shapes the final transport plan and determines which movements are most efficient.
Euclidean Distance: The straight-line length between two points in a multi-dimensional space, computed as the square root of the sum of squared differences between coordinates. It provides the most common “ground distance” for cost matrices in physical or geometric problems. This metric assumes a flat geometry and serves as the baseline for many higher-level optimal transport calculations.
Wasserstein-1 Distance: A metric that calculates the minimum cost of transforming one distribution into another using the absolute distance between points as the cost function. It scales linearly with distance, meaning it treats moving a single large unit of mass the same as moving many smaller units if the total displacement is identical. Often called the Earth Mover’s Distance, it is robust to outliers and frequently used in image processing.
Wasserstein-2 Distance: A transport metric that utilizes the square of the Euclidean distance as the cost for moving mass between points. By squaring the distance, it disproportionately penalizes long-range movements, which often results in unique, “smoother” transport plans that favor local shifts. It is the standard choice in many geometric deep learning applications because of its deep connections to Riemannian geometry and fluid dynamics.
Earth Mover’s Distance: An alternative name for the Wasserstein-1 distance that emphasizes the physical intuition of moving piles of “dirt” to fill “holes” with minimum work. It measures the total mass moved multiplied by the distance traveled, providing a meaningful divergence even when distributions do not overlap. This property makes it superior to traditional information-theoretic measures like KL-divergence for comparing disjoint datasets.
\(W_2\) Transport Plan: The specific mapping or “coupling” that achieves the minimum cost under a squared Euclidean penalty. Because it heavily discourages long-distance jumps, the resulting plan tends to preserve the local structure of the data more effectively than other metrics. This makes it a powerful tool for interpolating between shapes or distributions in a way that feels physically natural.
Normalization: The process of scaling a vector of non-negative values so that they sum exactly to one, effectively turning a set of raw weights into a valid probability distribution. This ensures that the total “mass” is conserved and consistent across different datasets regardless of their original scale. It is a mandatory preprocessing step in optimal transport to ensure that the total supply at the source matches the total demand at the destination.
Optimal Transport Plan: The specific mapping that defines exactly how much mass should be moved from each source location to each destination to achieve the minimum total cost. It is represented as a joint probability distribution where the entry at row \(i\) and column \(j\) indicates the amount of mass transferred between those two points. Finding this plan is the central objective of the primal transport problem, providing a clear visualization of how one distribution geometrically transforms into another.
Marginal Constraints: The requirement that the sum of all mass exiting a source point must equal its original weight, and the sum of all mass arriving at a destination must match its target weight. In the transport matrix, these constraints ensure that row sums and column sums align perfectly with the source and target distributions, respectively. They act as the conservation laws of the system, guaranteeing that no mass is created or destroyed during the redistribution process.
Regularized Transport Plan: A modified version of the transport map that incorporates an entropy penalty to “smooth” the resulting distribution of mass. This adjustment prevents the plan from becoming too sparse and allows for much faster computation using iterative algorithms like Sinkhorn’s. While it introduces a small amount of diffusion or “blur,” it makes the optimization problem strictly convex and numerically stable.
Kernel: A matrix where each entry \(K_{ij}\) represents the exponential of the negative cost scaled by a smoothing parameter, quantifying the “affinity” between two points. It acts as the core operator in regularized transport, effectively filtering the source distribution to see how easily its mass can reach different destination points. This transformation turns the constrained optimization problem into a series of simple matrix-vector multiplications.
Numerical Underflow: A computational failure that occurs when a value becomes so small that the hardware represents it as zero, losing all meaningful data. In optimal transport, this often happens when calculating the exponential of large negative costs during the kernel construction. Preventing underflow is the primary motivation for shifting calculations into the log-domain to maintain the integrity of the potentials.
Log-Domain: A computational approach where operations are performed on the logarithms of values rather than the values themselves to preserve precision across many orders of magnitude. By turning multiplications into additions and divisions into subtractions, this method avoids the precision loss associated with extremely small or large numbers. It is the standard environment for implementing stable Sinkhorn iterations in production-level code.
Epsilon (\(\epsilon\)): A smoothing parameter, often called “temperature,” that controls the trade-off between the accuracy of the transport distance and the speed of the computation. A high \(\epsilon\) produces a very blurry, entropic plan that is easy to solve, while a low \(\epsilon\) approaches the exact, sharp transport solution but becomes numerically difficult to handle. It serves as the primary dial for balancing physical realism with algorithmic performance.
Floating Point: A standardized system for representing real numbers in digital hardware using a significand and an exponent to cover a vast range of values. Because it has a finite number of bits, it can only approximate most real numbers, leading to tiny errors that can accumulate during complex iterations. Understanding these limitations is vital for implementing the stable mathematical operators required for high-dimensional transport problems.
Log-Sum-Exp (LSE): A specialized mathematical function designed to compute the logarithm of a sum of exponentiated values without ever explicitly calculating the large exponentials. It maintains numerical stability by factoring out the largest value in the set before performing the summation. This operator is the fundamental building block for performing stable Sinkhorn updates within the log-domain.
Dual Potentials: The variables \((f, g)\) used in the dual formulation of optimal transport that represent the relative “price” or “pressure” at each source and destination point. Rather than describing the movement of mass, they describe the geometry of the cost landscape and how it supports the optimal plan. In the Sinkhorn algorithm, these potentials are iteratively updated until the marginal constraints are satisfied.
Strong Duality: A mathematical property ensuring that the minimum cost of moving mass in the primal problem is exactly equal to the maximum “profit” or potential difference in the dual problem. When this condition holds, there is no duality gap, meaning the two seemingly different perspectives provide the same optimal value. It provides a powerful verification tool, as one can check the accuracy of a transport solution by comparing the primal and dual objectives.
Kantorovich Potentials: The pair of functions that maximize the dual objective, effectively assigning a value to each point such that their difference never exceeds the transport cost between them. They represent the “shadow prices” of the marginal constraints, indicating how much the total cost would change if mass was added to a specific location. These potentials are the primary output when solving the dual problem and are used to reconstruct the displacement interpolation.
Dual Problem: A reformulation of the optimal transport task that shifts the focus from finding a transport plan to finding a pair of potential functions that maximize a specific objective. This perspective is often easier to solve because it involves unconstrained optimization over vectors rather than a large matrix. It provides the mathematical foundation for understanding the “pressure” or “gradient” that drives mass from one distribution to another.
Primal Problem: The direct formulation of optimal transport that seeks the specific matrix of mass movements that minimizes the total weighted distance. It deals with the concrete physical constraints of the system, such as ensuring all mass is moved and no capacities are exceeded. While intuitive, it is often more computationally expensive to solve directly than its dual counterpart in high-dimensional settings.
Linear Interpolation: A method of blending two distributions by taking a weighted average of their intensities at every fixed coordinate. It does not account for the underlying geometry or move mass between locations, instead causing a “fading” effect where the source disappears as the target appears. It serves as the simplest baseline for transitions but fails to preserve the structural integrity of the objects being morphed.
Displacement Interpolation: A geometric method of creating intermediate distributions by physically shifting mass along the optimal paths defined by a transport plan. It follows the shortest path, or geodesic, in Wasserstein space, ensuring that the characteristics of the source distribution are preserved as they travel to the target. Often called McCann interpolation, it is the standard technique for creating fluid, motion-based animations and shape transitions.
Push-Forward: A mathematical operation that describes how a probability distribution is reshaped when its underlying points are moved by a specific function or mapping. It calculates the new distribution by tracking where every unit of mass ends up after the transformation is applied. This is the fundamental mechanism used to define how mass actually travels during a transport-based interpolation.
Interpolation: The process of constructing new data points or distributions that represent a smooth transition between two known endpoints. In the context of optimal transport, it involves finding a sequence of intermediate states that represent a “middle ground” between a source and a target. It allows researchers to explore the space between data categories, such as finding the average of two distinct facial expressions or medical images.
Transport Plan (\(\pi\)): A joint distribution matrix that serves as a detailed matching guide, specifying the exact quantity of mass to be transferred from every source point to every destination point. Each entry \(\pi_{ij}\) acts as a coupling that links a location in the first distribution to a location in the second. It is the primary output of the primal optimization problem and provides the routing instructions for displacement interpolation.
Convex Combination: A specific type of weighted average where the coefficients are non-negative and sum exactly to one. It ensures that the resulting intermediate state remains within the bounds of the original inputs and retains the properties of a valid probability distribution. This mathematical structure is used to define the progress of an interpolation at any given time step \(t\) between zero and one.
Mass Conservation: The fundamental physical constraint requiring that the total probability weight remains constant throughout the entire transport process. It ensures that every unit of mass leaving the source is accounted for at the destination, preventing any “leakage” or spontaneous creation of weight. This principle is strictly enforced by the marginal constraints and is essential for maintaining the validity of the distributions during morphing.
Morphing: The visual process of transforming one shape or image into another by combining changes in both position and intensity. Unlike simple blending, high-quality morphing in optimal transport involves “sliding” the mass through space to create a continuous and physically plausible motion. It is the practical application of displacement interpolation, used extensively in computer graphics and medical imaging to visualize deformation.

title: GNN Message Passing
text:
Phase 0 — Foundations
  └─ 0.1  Concatenation
  └─ 0.2  Broadcasting
  └─ 0.3  MLP mathematics
  └─ 0.4  scatter_add_ (index-based accumulation)

Phase 1 — The C₄ Graph as a Data Structure
  └─ 1.1  Nodes, edges, and feature matrices
  └─ 1.2  Edge index representation

Phase 2 — Message Computation
  └─ 2.1  What a message is and why it's formed this way
  └─ 2.2  Walking through m₀₁ and m₀₃ by hand

Phase 3 — Aggregation
  └─ 3.1  scatter_add_ on C₄ step by step
  └─ 3.2  Why identical neighbors collapse (WL preview)

Phase 4 — Node Update
  └─ 4.1  The update MLP on node 0 by hand

Phase 5 — Full Data Flow

  └─ 5.2  Intuition: what did one round of message passing actually do?




Graph: \(C_4\) — cycle on 4 nodes \(\{0,1,2,3\}\), edges \(\{(0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,0)\}\)
Node features: \(\mathbf{x} = [2, 1, 1, 1]^\top\)
The number of nodes: \(N = 4\).
\(\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times F}\) is the node feature matrix, where row \(i\) is the feature vector of node \(i\).
Scalar features, \(F=1\):
\[\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 1}\]







You know matrix multiplication joins matrices through inner dimensions. Concatenation is simpler — it’s just physically stacking values with no arithmetic.
Given two vectors:
\[\mathbf{a} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \end{bmatrix}\]
Their concatenation is:
\[[\mathbf{a} \| \mathbf{b}] = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\]
No multiplication, no addition. Just placing them side by side into one longer vector. The \(\|\) symbol means exactly that — join.
In our \(C_4\) example, node 0 has feature \(h_0 = 2\) and node 1 has feature \(h_1 = 1\). Before computing the message on edge \(0 \to 1\), we first form the input to the MLP:
\[[h_0 \| h_1] = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{m}_{01}\]
This is a 2-dimensional vector assembled from two 1-dimensional scalars. If nodes had, say, 4 features each, the concatenation would produce an 8-dimensional vector. The dimension simply adds.
\[\dim([\mathbf{a} \| \mathbf{b}]) = \dim(\mathbf{a}) + \dim(\mathbf{b})\]
That’s the entirety of concatenation.




Broadcasting answers one question: what do you do when an operation requires two tensors of matching shape, but they don’t match?
Let’s build the need for it concretely first.
1. The situation
After computing messages on all edges, you have a message matrix — one row per edge:
\[\text{messages} = \begin{bmatrix} m_{01} \\\\ m_{10} \\\\ m_{12} \\\\ m_{21} \\\\ m_{23} \\\\ m_{32} \\\\ m_{30} \\\\ m_{03} \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{shape: } [8 \times H]\]
where \(H\) is the hidden dimension (how many numbers represent each message). Say \(H = 4\) for concreteness.
Each message row needs to be sent to its destination node. The destination indices for these 8 edges are:
\[\text{dst} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 3 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 3 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{shape: } [8]\]
This says: message 0 goes to node 1, message 1 goes to node 0, message 2 goes to node 2, and so on.

2. The mismatch
To tell scatter_add_ where each message goes, it needs dst and messages to have the same shape \([8 \times 4]\).
But dst is shape \([8]\) — just a list of 8 numbers. messages is \([8 \times 4]\).
They don’t match. Broadcasting fixes this.

3. What broadcasting does
It repeats the smaller tensor along the missing dimension until shapes align:
\[\text{dst} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 3 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 3 \end{bmatrix} \xrightarrow{\text{expand to } [8 \times 4]} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\\\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\\\ 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\\\ 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\\\ 3 & 3 & 3 & 3 \\\\ 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\\\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\\\ 3 & 3 & 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix}\]
No new data is created. Each destination index is simply copied across all \(H\) columns — because the same index applies to every dimension of that message row.
This is what dst.unsqueeze(-1).expand_as(messages) does in code: - unsqueeze(-1) reshapes \([8]\) → \([8 \times 1]\) - expand_as(messages) stretches \([8 \times 1]\) → \([8 \times 4]\) by repeating
The core rule

A dimension of size 1 can be stretched to match any size — because repeating one value across positions costs no new information.


Revision
That’s actually not quite right — let me show you why before we move on.
unsqueeze(-1) does one thing only: it inserts a new dimension of size 1 at the last position.
So the transformation is:
\[[6] \xrightarrow{\text{unsqueeze(-1)}} [6 \times 1]\]
The result is — \([6 \times 1]\).
The expansion to \([6 \times 3]\) happens after, in the separate expand_as step. These are two distinct operations:



Operation
Input shape
Output shape




unsqueeze(-1)
\([6]\)
\([6 \times 1]\)


expand_as(messages)
\([6 \times 1]\)
\([6 \times 3]\)



The reason they’re separated: unsqueeze tells PyTorch where the new dimension goes, and expand_as tells it how far to stretch it. One step cannot do both.
Revisiting the original \(C_4\) case with \(H=4\):
\[[8] \xrightarrow{\text{unsqueeze(-1)}} [8 \times 1] \xrightarrow{\text{expand\_as}} [8 \times 4]\]




MLP is actually a strict subset of what a CNN does.
1. What you already know from CNN
Based on the concept of CNN, a convolutional layer does this to a spatial input:
\[\mathbf{y} = \text{ReLU}(\mathbf{W} * \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})\]
where \(*\) is the convolution — a sliding dot product across spatial positions.
Now ask: what if the input had no spatial dimensions at all — just a single vector?
The sliding window collapses to a single position. The convolution reduces to a plain dot product. That is an MLP layer.

2. One MLP layer
\[\mathbf{y} = \text{ReLU}(\mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})\]







Symbol
Meaning




\(\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{in}}\)
input vector


\(\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{out} \times d_{in}}\)
learned weight matrix


\(\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{out}}\)
learned bias vector


\(\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{out}}\)
output vector



ReLU is applied elementwise: \(\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)\).
Stack two such layers and you have a 2-layer MLP — exactly what \(\text{MLP}_\text{msg}\) and \(\text{MLP}_\text{update}\) are.

3. Concretely on edge \(0 \to 1\)
Input after concatenation: \(\mathbf{x} = [2, 1]^\top\), so \(d_{in} = 2\). Let \(d_{out} = H = 2\) for simplicity, with made-up weights:
\[\mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\\\ 0 \end{bmatrix}\]
Then:
\[\mathbf{m}_{01} = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\\\ 0 \end{bmatrix}\right) = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\]
That vector \([2, 1]^\top\) is the message on edge \(0 \to 1\).




1. The problem it solves
After computing all 8 messages on \(C_4\), you need to sum them into their destination nodes. Node 0 should receive messages from nodes 1 and 3. Node 1 should receive from nodes 0 and 2. And so on.
You have:
\[\text{messages} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{m}_{01} \\\\ \mathbf{m}_{10} \\\\ \mathbf{m}_{12} \\\\ \mathbf{m}_{21} \\\\ \mathbf{m}_{23} \\\\ \mathbf{m}_{32} \\\\ \mathbf{m}_{30} \\\\ \mathbf{m}_{03} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{dst} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 3 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 3 \end{bmatrix}\]
scatter_add_ reads dst row by row and adds each message into the corresponding row of an accumulator matrix agg of shape \([N \times H] = [4 \times 2]\), initialized to zero.

2. Step-by-step on \(C_4\)
\[\text{agg} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\]

Final result:
\[\text{agg} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 \\\\ 3 & 2 \\\\ 2 & 2 \\\\ 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\]
Row \(i\) of agg is exactly \(\mathbf{M}_i\) — the aggregated neighborhood summary for node \(i\).

3. How each operation built the final agg
agg starts as a \(4 \times 2\) zero matrix — one row per node:
\[\text{agg} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \leftarrow \text{node 0, 1, 2, 3}\]
scatter_add_ processes messages one at a time. Each message has a destination index — that index tells it which row to add into. It’s a running sum, not a replacement.
Tracing only node 0’s row to keep it tight:
\[\text{agg}[0] = \underbrace{[0,0]}_{\text{init}} \xrightarrow{+\,\mathbf{m}_{10}=[1,2]} [1,2] \xrightarrow{+\,\mathbf{m}_{30}=[1,2]} [2,4]\]
Two messages landed on row 0, both identical because \(h_1 = h_3\). Their sum is \([2,4]\).
The same accumulation runs independently for every other row — messages destined for node 1 pile into row 1, for node 2 into row 2, and so on. No row interferes with another.
Full final state:
\[\text{agg} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 \\\\ 3 & 2 \\\\ 2 & 2 \\\\ 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \leftarrow \mathbf{M}_0,\ \mathbf{M}_1,\ \mathbf{M}_2,\ \mathbf{M}_3\]








\(C_4\) is a cycle graph with 4 nodes and 4 edges. You may imagine its structure. The question is: how does a computer represent it?
Nodes are represented as rows in a feature matrix \(\mathbf{H}\):
\[\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{shape: } [4 \times 1]\]
Row \(i\) is the feature vector of node \(i\). Here each node has a single scalar feature, so \(F = 1\) and the matrix is \([N \times F] = [4 \times 1]\).
In general, if each node carried a 16-dimensional feature vector, \(\mathbf{H}\) would be \([4 \times 16]\), i.e., 4 nodes and 16 features. The structure is the same–rows are nodes, columns are features.




Edges are stored as a pair of index lists — source and destination:
\[\text{src} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 3 \\\\ 3 \\\\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{dst} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 3 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 3 \end{bmatrix}\]
Row \(k\) of both together describes one directed edge: \(\text{src}[k] \to \text{dst}[k]\). \(C_4\) has 4 undirected edges, each stored twice — once per direction — giving \(2E = 8\) rows.
Visually:
\[0 \leftrightarrow 1 \leftrightarrow 2 \leftrightarrow 3 \leftrightarrow 0\]
Every node has exactly 2 neighbors. That means every node will receive exactly 2 messages per round — which you already saw in the scatter_add_ table.

Question: If \(C_4\) had edge features \(\mathbf{e}_{ij}\) of dimension \(F_e = 3\), what would the shape of the edge feature matrix be?
Not quite — let’s think through it carefully.
The edge feature matrix stores one feature vector per edge. How many edges does \(C_4\) have in its directed representation?
4 undirected edges, each stored twice, giving \(2E = 8\) directed edges.
So the matrix needs 8 rows — one per directed edge — and 3 columns for the \(F_e = 3\) features.
That makes the answer : \([8 \times 3]\).

Anything indexed by nodes has \(N\) rows. Anything indexed by edges has \(2E\) rows.


Question: The message matrix (output of \(\text{MLP}_\text{msg}\)) lives in which category — node-indexed or edge-indexed — and what is its shape on \(C_4\) with \(H = 4\)?

The MLP\(_\text{msg}\) takes one concatenated input per edge and produces one output per edge. So is the output indexed by nodes or by edges?

And separately:

On \(C_4\) with \(2E = 8\) directed edges and hidden dimension \(H = 4\), how many rows must the message matrix have?

Using those two observations — we get \([8 \times 4]\) shape.
