Every shape in the full data flow reduces to one of these two categories — node-indexed or edge-indexed. Keep that table in mind as we proceed.








From CNN — a convolutional filter looks at a local neighborhood of pixels to extract spatial patterns. Message passing is the graph analogue: each edge carries information from its local neighborhood — the two endpoint nodes — into a learned representation.
Formally, for every directed edge \(i \to j\):
\[\mathbf{m}_{ij} = \text{MLP}_\text{msg}([\mathbf{h}_i \| \mathbf{h}_j])\]
The input is the concatenation of both endpoints. Why both? Because the message should capture not just who is sending, but the relationship between sender and receiver. This is strictly richer than classic GCN which only uses \(\mathbf{h}_i\).




For \(C_4\) with \(\mathbf{h} = [2,1,1,1]^\top\) and the same identity weights from Phase 0.3:
\[\mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\\\ 0 \end{bmatrix}\]
Edge \(0 \to 1\):
\[\mathbf{m}_{01} = \text{ReLU}\left(\mathbf{W}[h_0 \| h_1] + \mathbf{b}\right) = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix}1&0 \\\\ 0&1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 1\end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 1\end{bmatrix}\]
Edge \(0 \to 3\):
\[\mathbf{m}_{03} = \text{ReLU}\left(\mathbf{W}[h_0 \| h_3] + \mathbf{b}\right) = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix}1&0 \\\\ 0&1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 1\end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 1\end{bmatrix}\]
Identical inputs → identical outputs. This is not a coincidence — it is a structural fact about \(C_4\).

1. The WL Limit — First Encounter
Node 0 sends the same message to both its neighbors because \(h_1 = h_3 = 1\). Now flip the question:

From node 0’s perspective — can it tell nodes 1 and 3 apart based on what it sends them?

It cannot. And more critically — when node 0 receives messages back from nodes 1 and 3 in the next phase, those will also be identical. Node 0 cannot distinguish its two neighbors.
This is the Weisfeiler-Lehman expressiveness limit: if two nodes have identical neighborhood structure and features, no standard message passing GNN can distinguish them. We’ll see this collapse explicitly in Phase 3.








You’ve already seen this mechanics in Phase 0.4. Now we run it with real messages from Phase 2.

Accumulating into agg by destination:
\[\mathbf{M}_0 = \mathbf{m}_{10} + \mathbf{m}_{30} = [1,2]+[1,2] = [2,4]\] \[\mathbf{M}_1 = \mathbf{m}_{01} + \mathbf{m}_{21} = [2,1]+[1,1] = [3,2]\] \[\mathbf{M}_2 = \mathbf{m}_{12} + \mathbf{m}_{32} = [1,1]+[1,1] = [2,2]\] \[\mathbf{M}_3 = \mathbf{m}_{23} + \mathbf{m}_{03} = [1,1]+[2,1] = [3,2]\]
\[\text{agg} = \begin{bmatrix} 2&4 \\\\ 3&2 \\\\ 2&2 \\\\ 3&2 \end{bmatrix}\]




Look at rows 1 and 3:
\[\mathbf{M}_1 = \mathbf{M}_3 = [3,2]\]
After one full round of message passing, nodes 1 and 3 have identical aggregated summaries. Any GNN update applied to these identical \(\mathbf{M}\) values will produce identical updated features — the network provably cannot distinguish them.
This isn’t a weight-tuning problem. No training fixes it. It’s a structural limitation — the neighborhood of node 1 and node 3 look identical from the graph’s perspective.








Before the math — think about what \(\mathbf{M}_i\) alone contains.
\(\mathbf{M}_0 = [2,4]\) summarizes node 0’s neighborhood. But it contains no information about node 0 itself. If you replaced \(\mathbf{h}_0\) with \(\mathbf{M}_0\) directly, node 0 would forget it ever had feature \(h_0 = 2\).
The fix is to concatenate before updating:
\[\mathbf{h}_i' = \text{MLP}_\text{update}([\mathbf{h}_i \| \mathbf{M}_i])\]
The node retains its own identity alongside its neighborhood summary. The update MLP then learns how to blend the two.




For node 0, using the same identity weights and \(H=2\), but now \(d_{in} = 3\) because \([\mathbf{h}_0 \| \mathbf{M}_0] = [2 \| 2, 4] = [2, 2, 4]^\top\):
\[\mathbf{W}_\text{update} = \begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0 \\\\ 0&1&0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix}0 \\\\ 0\end{bmatrix}\]
\[\mathbf{h}_0' = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0 \\\\ 0&1&0\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 4\end{bmatrix}\right) = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 2\end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 2\end{bmatrix}\]

Question: Node 2 received from nodes 1 and 3, both with \(h=1\). Why is \(\mathbf{h}_2'\) different from \(\mathbf{h}_1'\) despite all three nodes starting with \(h=1\)?
Because, node 2’s neighbors both have \(h=1\), while node 1’s neighbors have \(h=2\) and \(h=1\)

On \(\mathbf{W}_\text{update}\)
\(\mathbf{W}_\text{update}\) — was assummed to be such and chosen deliberately to keep the arithmetic transparent. In a real GNN, \(\mathbf{W}_\text{update}\) is learned during training via backpropagation, just like CNN filter weights.
The \([2 \times 3]\) shape comes directly from the dimensions of the input and output:
\[\mathbf{W}_\text{update} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{out} \times d_{in}}\]
Here:

\(d_{in} = 3\) because \([\mathbf{h}_i \| \mathbf{M}_i]\) concatenates a scalar \(\mathbf{h}_i \in \mathbb{R}^1\) with \(\mathbf{M}_i \in \mathbb{R}^2\), giving a 3-dimensional input
\(d_{out} = 2\) because we chose hidden dimension \(H = 2\)

So the matrix must be \([2 \times 3]\) to satisfy the matrix multiplication \(\mathbf{W}_\text{update} \cdot [\mathbf{h}_i \| \mathbf{M}_i]\):
\[[2 \times 3] \cdot [3 \times 1] = [2 \times 1]\]
The general rule — consistent with everything you’ve seen so far:
\[\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{out} \times d_{in}}, \quad d_{in} = F + H, \quad d_{out} = H\]








Every transformation in one pipeline, using \(C_4\) values throughout:
\[[N, F] \xrightarrow{\text{gather}} [2E,\ 2F] \xrightarrow{\text{MLP}_\text{msg}} [2E, H] \xrightarrow{\text{scatter\_add}} [N, H] \xrightarrow{\text{MLP}_\text{update}} [N, H]\]
Substituting \(N=4,\ F=1,\ E=4,\ H=2\):
\[[4,1] \xrightarrow{\text{gather}} [8,2] \xrightarrow{\text{MLP}_\text{msg}} [8,2] \xrightarrow{\text{scatter\_add}} [4,2] \xrightarrow{\text{MLP}_\text{update}} [4,2]\]
Each arrow is one operation. Let’s nail what happens at each boundary.

Step 1 — Gather: \([4,1] \to [8,2]\)
For every directed edge, look up the features of its source and destination node from \(\mathbf{H}\), then concatenate them:
\[\text{edge } k: \quad [\mathbf{h}_{\text{src}[k]} \| \mathbf{h}_{\text{dst}[k]}]\]
8 edges × 2 concatenated scalars = \([8 \times 2]\). No arithmetic yet — pure lookup and concatenation.

Step 2 — \(\text{MLP}_\text{msg}\): \([8,2] \to [8,2]\)
Each of the 8 rows passes independently through the same MLP. Shape stays \([8 \times 2]\) because \(d_{in} = 2F = 2\) and \(d_{out} = H = 2\).

Step 3 — scatter_add_: \([8,2] \to [4,2]\)
8 edge-indexed messages collapse into 4 node-indexed aggregations. Edge dimension \(\to\) node dimension. This is the only step that reduces the number of rows.

Step 4 — \(\text{MLP}_\text{update}\): \([4,2] \to [4,2]\)
Each node concatenates its own feature \(\mathbf{h}_i \in \mathbb{R}^1\) with \(\mathbf{M}_i \in \mathbb{R}^2\) internally, giving \(d_{in} = 3\), \(d_{out} = H = 2\). The 4 nodes pass independently through the same MLP. Output is the new feature matrix \(\mathbf{H}' \in [4 \times 2]\).




Before message passing, every node knew only itself:
\[\mathbf{h}_0 = 2, \quad \mathbf{h}_1 = \mathbf{h}_2 = \mathbf{h}_3 = 1\]
After one round, every node knows itself plus a summary of its immediate neighbors:
\[\mathbf{h}_0' = [2,2], \quad \mathbf{h}_1' = \mathbf{h}_3' = [1,3], \quad \mathbf{h}_2' = [1,2]\]
Each updated feature vector encodes a 1-hop neighborhood summary. Stack another round on top, and every node would encode a 2-hop summary. This is how GNNs build up global graph awareness — one hop at a time.

Question: The table shows node 0 on \(C_4\) sees nodes 1 and 3 after layer 1, then node 2 after layer 2. Why does it take two layers to reach node 2, not one?
Receptive field grows by exactly one hop per layer.
Node 0’s neighbors are \(\{1, 3\}\) — one hop away. Node 2 is two hops away via \(0\to1\to2\) or \(0\to3\to2\). One layer reaches one hop. Two layers reach two hops. This is purely graph distance.
This is called the receptive field — directly analogous to CNN receptive field, where stacking convolutional layers expands the spatial region each output pixel “sees.”
Question: The portion says mean pooling is preferred over sum because it’s invariant to graph size. Can you think of a scenario where sum pooling would actually lose information that mean pooling preserves, or vice versa?

Graph A: 3 nodes, all with feature value 1 \(\to\) sum = 3; mean = 1
Graph B: 6 nodes, all with feature value 1 \(\to\) sum = 6; mean = 1

If energy scales with atom count, sum is the natural choice — bigger molecule, larger sum, larger prediction. Mean would wash that out.
QM9 uses mean specifically because the model handles variable-size molecules and needs stable training — scale invariance is prioritized over size-sensitivity. Neither is universally correct — it’s a design choice driven by the task.

The new concepts to focus are:







Concept
Core idea




Receptive field
depth = hops seen


Global pooling
nodes → single graph vector


Permutation invariance
addition commutes, so node ordering doesn’t matter











You have raw node features \(\mathbf{X}\) going into the network. On QM9, each atom carries 11 chemically meaningful features — atom type, charge, etc. On \(C_4\), each node carries a scalar, so \(F=1\).
The problem: every MPNN layer operates in a uniform hidden space of dimension \(H=64\). You cannot feed a 1-dimensional or 11-dimensional vector directly into a layer expecting 64-dimensional inputs.
The fix is a single linear projection — one MLP layer with no hidden depth:
\[\mathbf{H}^{(0)} = \text{ReLU}(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}_\text{in})\]



Symbol
Shape
Meaning




\(\mathbf{X}\)
\([N \times F]\)
raw features


\(\mathbf{W}_\text{in}\)
\([F \times H]\)
learned projection


\(\mathbf{H}^{(0)}\)
\([N \times H]\)
projected features



On \(C_4\) with \(F=1\), \(H=2\) for simplicity:
\[\mathbf{W}_\text{in} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\]
\[\mathbf{H}^{(0)} = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1&1\end{bmatrix}\right) = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix}2&2 \\\\ 1&1 \\\\ 1&1 \\\\ 1&1\end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix}2&2 \\\\ 1&1 \\\\ 1&1 \\\\ 1&1\end{bmatrix}\]
Every node is now living in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) — same space, ready for message passing.

Correct. The matrix multiplication requires:
\[[N \times F] \cdot [F \times H] = [N \times H]\]
so \(\mathbf{W}_\text{in}\) must be \([F \times H] = [11 \times 64]\) — inner dimensions must match.








Every node now lives in a uniform \(H\)-dimensional space. A single MPNN layer — everything you built in Phases 2–4 — expands each node’s awareness by exactly one hop. Stacking \(L\) layers expands it by \(L\) hops.
Formally, each layer takes the current hidden state and produces the next:
\[\mathbf{H}^{(l+1)} = \text{MPNNLayer}\left(\mathbf{H}^{(l)},\ \mathcal{E}\right)\]
The superscript tracks depth, not time. \(\mathbf{H}^{(0)}\) is the projection output. \(\mathbf{H}^{(1)}\) encodes 1-hop neighborhoods. \(\mathbf{H}^{(2)}\) encodes 2-hop neighborhoods. And so on.




Tracing node 0 specifically across \(L=3\) layers:
Layer 0 → 1: Node 0 aggregates from \(\{1, 3\}\). Its new state \(\mathbf{h}_0^{(1)}\) encodes itself plus its direct neighbors.
Layer 1 → 2: Now \(\mathbf{h}_1^{(1)}\) already encodes node 1’s neighborhood \(\{0,2\}\). When node 0 aggregates from node 1 at layer 2, it indirectly pulls in node 2’s information. Node 0 has now seen 2 hops.
Layer 2 → 3: \(\mathbf{h}_2^{(2)}\) already encodes node 2’s neighborhood \(\{1,3\}\), which itself encoded \(\{0,2\}\). Node 0 now sees the full graph.
The receptive field table makes this concrete:



Layer
Node 0 sees




0
\(\{0\}\)


1
\(\{0,1,3\}\)


2
\(\{0,1,2,3\}\)


3
full graph



\(C_4\) has diameter 2 — any node reaches any other in at most 2 hops — so \(L=2\) already saturates the receptive field here. Layer 3 adds no new nodes but re-encodes richer higher-order structure.




This is exactly your CNN receptive field intuition:



CNN
GNN




Stacking conv layers
Stacking MPNN layers


Spatial neighborhood grows
Graph neighborhood grows


Kernel size controls hop width
Aggregation controls hop width


Deep = sees more pixels
Deep = sees more nodes



The difference: CNN neighborhoods are regular grids. GNN neighborhoods are irregular graphs — each node can have a different number of neighbors.

Question: On a path graph \(0\leftrightarrow1\leftrightarrow2\leftrightarrow3\leftrightarrow4\) with \(L=2\) layers, which nodes can node 0 see?
The node 0 can see: \(\{0,1,2\}\).

Correct — two hops from node 0 on a path graph reaches nodes \(\{0,1,2\}\). Node 3 is 3 hops away, node 4 is 4 hops — both out of reach with \(L=2\).








After \(L\) layers, you have \(\mathbf{H}^{(L)} \in [N \times H]\) — one rich vector per node. But the task is graph-level regression: predict a single number \(U_0\) for the entire molecule.
You need to compress \(N\) node vectors into one graph vector. This is pooling — same concept as spatial pooling in CNN, but over graph nodes instead of pixel grids.




\[\mathbf{g} = \frac{1}{4}\left(\mathbf{h}_0^{(L)} + \mathbf{h}_1^{(L)} + \mathbf{h}_2^{(L)} + \mathbf{h}_3^{(L)}\right)\]
Using our \(H=2\) updated states from Phase 4:
\[\mathbf{g} = \frac{1}{4}\left(\begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 2\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}1 \\\\3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}1 \\\\ 2\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}1 \\\\ 3\end{bmatrix}\right) = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix}5 \\\\ 10\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1.25 \\\\ 2.5\end{bmatrix}\]
One vector summarizing the entire graph.




In practice, a training batch contains multiple graphs packed together. Nodes from different graphs sit in the same matrix but must pool separately.
The batch tensor handles this — it maps every node to its graph index:
\[\texttt{batch} = [0,0,0,1,1]\]
This says nodes \(\{0,1,2\}\) belong to graph 0, nodes \(\{3,4\}\) belong to graph 1. scatter_add_ then accumulates by graph index — exactly the same mechanic as Phase 0.4, but now the index is a graph label instead of a node label.
\[\mathbf{g}_0 = \frac{\mathbf{h}_0+\mathbf{h}_1+\mathbf{h}_2}{3}, \quad \mathbf{g}_1 = \frac{\mathbf{h}_3+\mathbf{h}_4}{2}\]
Two graphs, two pooled vectors, computed in one pass.

Question: If a batch contains three graphs with 4, 2, and 6 nodes respectively, what does batch look like and what shape is the pooled output?
One entry per node, grouped by graph, one pooled vector per graph, i.e.:
batch \(= [0,0,0,0,1,1,2,2,2,2,2,2]\), output \([3 \times H]\)








You now have a single graph vector \(\mathbf{g} \in \mathbb{R}^H\) summarizing the entire molecule.
You have \(\mathbf{g} \in \mathbb{R}^{64}\) — a rich graph-level summary. The final step compresses it to a single scalar \(\hat{y}\):
\[\hat{y} = \mathbf{W}_2\,\text{ReLU}(\mathbf{W}_1\mathbf{g} + \mathbf{b}_1) + \mathbf{b}_2\]
Two MLP layers — nothing new mathematically. Just the same affine-then-nonlinearity chain from Phase 0.3, applied twice.
Tracing shapes with \(H=2\) and an intermediate dimension of 2:








Step
Operation
Shape




Input
\(\mathbf{g}\)
\([2]\)


Layer 1
\(\mathbf{W}_1\mathbf{g}+\mathbf{b}_1\)
\([2\times2]\cdot[2]=[2]\)


ReLU
elementwise
\([2]\)


Layer 2
\(\mathbf{W}_2(\cdot)+\mathbf{b}_2\)
\([1\times2]\cdot[2]=[1]\)


Output
\(\hat{y}\)
scalar







This is the final architectural guarantee worth understanding precisely.
Suppose you relabel \(C_4\)’s nodes — swap node 0 and node 2. Does \(\hat{y}\) change?
It doesn’t — and here’s exactly why, tracing back through the pipeline:

Mean pooling sums node vectors and divides. Addition commutes — order of summation is irrelevant. \(\mathbf{g}\) is identical regardless of node ordering.
MPNN layers aggregate via scatter_add_ — also a sum, also commutative.
Readout MLP is deterministic given \(\mathbf{g}\) — same input, same output.

The invariance is structural, not learned. No amount of training could break it — it is baked into the choice of sum/mean as the aggregation operator.
This matters physically: a molecule’s energy \(U_0\) is a property of its atomic structure, not of how you number its atoms.






Putting everything together in one trace:
\[\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1\end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{X}\ [4\times1]} \xrightarrow{\mathbf{W}_\text{in}} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix}2&2 \\\\ 1&1 \\\\ 1&1 \\\\ 1&1\end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{H}^{(0)}\ [4\times2]} \xrightarrow{L\times\text{MPNN}} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix}2&2 \\\\ 1&3 \\\\ 1&2 \\\\ 1&3\end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{H}^{(L)}\ [4\times2]} \xrightarrow{\text{mean}} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix}1.25 \\\\ 2.5\end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{g}\ [2]} \xrightarrow{\text{MLP}} \underbrace{\hat{y}}_{\text{scalar}}\]
Every shape transition follows one rule: node-indexed \([N\times H]\) or edge-indexed \([2E\times H]\), collapsing to a single vector at pooling.

objectID: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#setup
href: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#setup
title: GNN Message Passing
text:
Graph: \(C_4\) — cycle on 4 nodes \(\{0,1,2,3\}\), edges \(\{(0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,0)\}\)
Node features: \(\mathbf{x} = [2, 1, 1, 1]^\top\)
The number of nodes: \(N = 4\).
\(\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times F}\) is the node feature matrix, where row \(i\) is the feature vector of node \(i\).
Scalar features, \(F=1\):
\[\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 1}\]

objectID: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-0---foundations
href: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-0---foundations
title: GNN Message Passing
text:
You know matrix multiplication joins matrices through inner dimensions. Concatenation is simpler — it’s just physically stacking values with no arithmetic.
Given two vectors:
\[\mathbf{a} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \end{bmatrix}\]
Their concatenation is:
\[[\mathbf{a} \| \mathbf{b}] = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\]
No multiplication, no addition. Just placing them side by side into one longer vector. The \(\|\) symbol means exactly that — join.
In our \(C_4\) example, node 0 has feature \(h_0 = 2\) and node 1 has feature \(h_1 = 1\). Before computing the message on edge \(0 \to 1\), we first form the input to the MLP:
\[[h_0 \| h_1] = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{m}_{01}\]
This is a 2-dimensional vector assembled from two 1-dimensional scalars. If nodes had, say, 4 features each, the concatenation would produce an 8-dimensional vector. The dimension simply adds.
\[\dim([\mathbf{a} \| \mathbf{b}]) = \dim(\mathbf{a}) + \dim(\mathbf{b})\]
That’s the entirety of concatenation.




Broadcasting answers one question: what do you do when an operation requires two tensors of matching shape, but they don’t match?
Let’s build the need for it concretely first.
1. The situation
After computing messages on all edges, you have a message matrix — one row per edge:
\[\text{messages} = \begin{bmatrix} m_{01} \\\\ m_{10} \\\\ m_{12} \\\\ m_{21} \\\\ m_{23} \\\\ m_{32} \\\\ m_{30} \\\\ m_{03} \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{shape: } [8 \times H]\]
where \(H\) is the hidden dimension (how many numbers represent each message). Say \(H = 4\) for concreteness.
Each message row needs to be sent to its destination node. The destination indices for these 8 edges are:
\[\text{dst} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 3 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 3 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{shape: } [8]\]
This says: message 0 goes to node 1, message 1 goes to node 0, message 2 goes to node 2, and so on.

2. The mismatch
To tell scatter_add_ where each message goes, it needs dst and messages to have the same shape \([8 \times 4]\).
But dst is shape \([8]\) — just a list of 8 numbers. messages is \([8 \times 4]\).
They don’t match. Broadcasting fixes this.

3. What broadcasting does
It repeats the smaller tensor along the missing dimension until shapes align:
\[\text{dst} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 3 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 3 \end{bmatrix} \xrightarrow{\text{expand to } [8 \times 4]} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\\\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\\\ 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\\\ 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\\\ 3 & 3 & 3 & 3 \\\\ 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\\\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\\\ 3 & 3 & 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix}\]
No new data is created. Each destination index is simply copied across all \(H\) columns — because the same index applies to every dimension of that message row.
This is what dst.unsqueeze(-1).expand_as(messages) does in code: - unsqueeze(-1) reshapes \([8]\) → \([8 \times 1]\) - expand_as(messages) stretches \([8 \times 1]\) → \([8 \times 4]\) by repeating
The core rule

A dimension of size 1 can be stretched to match any size — because repeating one value across positions costs no new information.


Revision
That’s actually not quite right — let me show you why before we move on.
unsqueeze(-1) does one thing only: it inserts a new dimension of size 1 at the last position.
So the transformation is:
\[[6] \xrightarrow{\text{unsqueeze(-1)}} [6 \times 1]\]
The result is — \([6 \times 1]\).
The expansion to \([6 \times 3]\) happens after, in the separate expand_as step. These are two distinct operations:



Operation
Input shape
Output shape




unsqueeze(-1)
\([6]\)
\([6 \times 1]\)


expand_as(messages)
\([6 \times 1]\)
\([6 \times 3]\)



The reason they’re separated: unsqueeze tells PyTorch where the new dimension goes, and expand_as tells it how far to stretch it. One step cannot do both.
Revisiting the original \(C_4\) case with \(H=4\):
\[[8] \xrightarrow{\text{unsqueeze(-1)}} [8 \times 1] \xrightarrow{\text{expand\_as}} [8 \times 4]\]




MLP is actually a strict subset of what a CNN does.
1. What you already know from CNN
Based on the concept of CNN, a convolutional layer does this to a spatial input:
\[\mathbf{y} = \text{ReLU}(\mathbf{W} * \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})\]
where \(*\) is the convolution — a sliding dot product across spatial positions.
Now ask: what if the input had no spatial dimensions at all — just a single vector?
The sliding window collapses to a single position. The convolution reduces to a plain dot product. That is an MLP layer.

2. One MLP layer
\[\mathbf{y} = \text{ReLU}(\mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})\]







Symbol
Meaning




\(\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{in}}\)
input vector


\(\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{out} \times d_{in}}\)
learned weight matrix


\(\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{out}}\)
learned bias vector


\(\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{out}}\)
output vector



ReLU is applied elementwise: \(\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)\).
Stack two such layers and you have a 2-layer MLP — exactly what \(\text{MLP}_\text{msg}\) and \(\text{MLP}_\text{update}\) are.

3. Concretely on edge \(0 \to 1\)
Input after concatenation: \(\mathbf{x} = [2, 1]^\top\), so \(d_{in} = 2\). Let \(d_{out} = H = 2\) for simplicity, with made-up weights:
\[\mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\\\ 0 \end{bmatrix}\]
Then:
\[\mathbf{m}_{01} = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\\\ 0 \end{bmatrix}\right) = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\]
That vector \([2, 1]^\top\) is the message on edge \(0 \to 1\).




1. The problem it solves
After computing all 8 messages on \(C_4\), you need to sum them into their destination nodes. Node 0 should receive messages from nodes 1 and 3. Node 1 should receive from nodes 0 and 2. And so on.
You have:
\[\text{messages} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{m}_{01} \\\\ \mathbf{m}_{10} \\\\ \mathbf{m}_{12} \\\\ \mathbf{m}_{21} \\\\ \mathbf{m}_{23} \\\\ \mathbf{m}_{32} \\\\ \mathbf{m}_{30} \\\\ \mathbf{m}_{03} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{dst} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 3 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 3 \end{bmatrix}\]
scatter_add_ reads dst row by row and adds each message into the corresponding row of an accumulator matrix agg of shape \([N \times H] = [4 \times 2]\), initialized to zero.

2. Step-by-step on \(C_4\)
\[\text{agg} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\]



Step
Message
dst
Operation




1
\(\mathbf{m}_{01} = [2,1]\)
1
agg[1] += [2,1]


2
\(\mathbf{m}_{10} = [1,2]\)
0
agg[0] += [1,2]


3
\(\mathbf{m}_{12} = [1,1]\)
2
agg[2] += [1,1]


4
\(\mathbf{m}_{21} = [1,1]\)
1
agg[1] += [1,1]


5
\(\mathbf{m}_{23} = [1,1]\)
3
agg[3] += [1,1]


6
\(\mathbf{m}_{32} = [1,1]\)
2
agg[2] += [1,1]


7
\(\mathbf{m}_{30} = [1,2]\)
0
agg[0] += [1,2]


8
\(\mathbf{m}_{03} = [2,1]\)
3
agg[3] += [2,1]



Final result:
\[\text{agg} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 \\\\ 3 & 2 \\\\ 2 & 2 \\\\ 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\]
Row \(i\) of agg is exactly \(\mathbf{M}_i\) — the aggregated neighborhood summary for node \(i\).

3. How each operation built the final agg
agg starts as a \(4 \times 2\) zero matrix — one row per node:
\[\text{agg} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \leftarrow \text{node 0, 1, 2, 3}\]
scatter_add_ processes messages one at a time. Each message has a destination index — that index tells it which row to add into. It’s a running sum, not a replacement.
Tracing only node 0’s row to keep it tight:
\[\text{agg}[0] = \underbrace{[0,0]}_{\text{init}} \xrightarrow{+\,\mathbf{m}_{10}=[1,2]} [1,2] \xrightarrow{+\,\mathbf{m}_{30}=[1,2]} [2,4]\]
Two messages landed on row 0, both identical because \(h_1 = h_3\). Their sum is \([2,4]\).
The same accumulation runs independently for every other row — messages destined for node 1 pile into row 1, for node 2 into row 2, and so on. No row interferes with another.
Full final state:
\[\text{agg} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 \\\\ 3 & 2 \\\\ 2 & 2 \\\\ 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \leftarrow \mathbf{M}_0,\ \mathbf{M}_1,\ \mathbf{M}_2,\ \mathbf{M}_3\]

objectID: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-1---c_4-as-a-data-structure
href: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-1---c_4-as-a-data-structure
title: GNN Message Passing
text:
\(C_4\) is a cycle graph with 4 nodes and 4 edges. You may imagine its structure. The question is: how does a computer represent it?
Nodes are represented as rows in a feature matrix \(\mathbf{H}\):
\[\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{shape: } [4 \times 1]\]
Row \(i\) is the feature vector of node \(i\). Here each node has a single scalar feature, so \(F = 1\) and the matrix is \([N \times F] = [4 \times 1]\).
In general, if each node carried a 16-dimensional feature vector, \(\mathbf{H}\) would be \([4 \times 16]\), i.e., 4 nodes and 16 features. The structure is the same–rows are nodes, columns are features.




Edges are stored as a pair of index lists — source and destination:
\[\text{src} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 3 \\\\ 3 \\\\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{dst} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 3 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 0 \\\\ 3 \end{bmatrix}\]
Row \(k\) of both together describes one directed edge: \(\text{src}[k] \to \text{dst}[k]\). \(C_4\) has 4 undirected edges, each stored twice — once per direction — giving \(2E = 8\) rows.
Visually:
\[0 \leftrightarrow 1 \leftrightarrow 2 \leftrightarrow 3 \leftrightarrow 0\]
Every node has exactly 2 neighbors. That means every node will receive exactly 2 messages per round — which you already saw in the scatter_add_ table.

Question: If \(C_4\) had edge features \(\mathbf{e}_{ij}\) of dimension \(F_e = 3\), what would the shape of the edge feature matrix be?
Not quite — let’s think through it carefully.
The edge feature matrix stores one feature vector per edge. How many edges does \(C_4\) have in its directed representation?
4 undirected edges, each stored twice, giving \(2E = 8\) directed edges.
So the matrix needs 8 rows — one per directed edge — and 3 columns for the \(F_e = 3\) features.
That makes the answer : \([8 \times 3]\).

Anything indexed by nodes has \(N\) rows. Anything indexed by edges has \(2E\) rows.


Question: The message matrix (output of \(\text{MLP}_\text{msg}\)) lives in which category — node-indexed or edge-indexed — and what is its shape on \(C_4\) with \(H = 4\)?

The MLP\(_\text{msg}\) takes one concatenated input per edge and produces one output per edge. So is the output indexed by nodes or by edges?

And separately:

On \(C_4\) with \(2E = 8\) directed edges and hidden dimension \(H = 4\), how many rows must the message matrix have?

Using those two observations — we get \([8 \times 4]\) shape.

Revision
You now have the full data structure picture:



Object
Shape
Indexed by




\(\mathbf{H}\)
\([4 \times 1]\)
nodes


src, dst
\([8]\)
directed edges


messages
\([8 \times H]\)
directed edges


agg \(\mathbf{M}\)
\([4 \times H]\)
nodes



Every shape in the full data flow reduces to one of these two categories — node-indexed or edge-indexed. Keep that table in mind as we proceed.

objectID: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-2---message-computation
href: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-2---message-computation
title: GNN Message Passing
text:
From CNN — a convolutional filter looks at a local neighborhood of pixels to extract spatial patterns. Message passing is the graph analogue: each edge carries information from its local neighborhood — the two endpoint nodes — into a learned representation.
Formally, for every directed edge \(i \to j\):
\[\mathbf{m}_{ij} = \text{MLP}_\text{msg}([\mathbf{h}_i \| \mathbf{h}_j])\]
The input is the concatenation of both endpoints. Why both? Because the message should capture not just who is sending, but the relationship between sender and receiver. This is strictly richer than classic GCN which only uses \(\mathbf{h}_i\).




For \(C_4\) with \(\mathbf{h} = [2,1,1,1]^\top\) and the same identity weights from Phase 0.3:
\[\mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\\\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\\\ 0 \end{bmatrix}\]
Edge \(0 \to 1\):
\[\mathbf{m}_{01} = \text{ReLU}\left(\mathbf{W}[h_0 \| h_1] + \mathbf{b}\right) = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix}1&0 \\\\ 0&1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 1\end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 1\end{bmatrix}\]
Edge \(0 \to 3\):
\[\mathbf{m}_{03} = \text{ReLU}\left(\mathbf{W}[h_0 \| h_3] + \mathbf{b}\right) = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix}1&0 \\\\ 0&1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 1\end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 1\end{bmatrix}\]
Identical inputs → identical outputs. This is not a coincidence — it is a structural fact about \(C_4\).

1. The WL Limit — First Encounter
Node 0 sends the same message to both its neighbors because \(h_1 = h_3 = 1\). Now flip the question:

From node 0’s perspective — can it tell nodes 1 and 3 apart based on what it sends them?

It cannot. And more critically — when node 0 receives messages back from nodes 1 and 3 in the next phase, those will also be identical. Node 0 cannot distinguish its two neighbors.
This is the Weisfeiler-Lehman expressiveness limit: if two nodes have identical neighborhood structure and features, no standard message passing GNN can distinguish them. We’ll see this collapse explicitly in Phase 3.

objectID: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-3---aggregation
href: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-3---aggregation
title: GNN Message Passing
text:
You’ve already seen this mechanics in Phase 0.4. Now we run it with real messages from Phase 2.
First, the full message matrix. Using identity weights, each message \(\mathbf{m}_{ij} = [h_i, h_j]^\top\):



Edge
src → dst
Message \((m_{src \to dst})\)




\(0\to1\)
0 → 1
\([2,1]\)


\(1\to0\)
1 → 0
\([1,2]\)


\(1\to2\)
1 → 2
\([1,1]\)


\(2\to1\)
2 → 1
\([1,1]\)


\(2\to3\)
2 → 3
\([1,1]\)


\(3\to2\)
3 → 2
\([1,1]\)


\(3\to0\)
3 → 0
\([1,2]\)


\(0\to3\)
0 → 3
\([2,1]\)



Accumulating into agg by destination:
\[\mathbf{M}_0 = \mathbf{m}_{10} + \mathbf{m}_{30} = [1,2]+[1,2] = [2,4]\] \[\mathbf{M}_1 = \mathbf{m}_{01} + \mathbf{m}_{21} = [2,1]+[1,1] = [3,2]\] \[\mathbf{M}_2 = \mathbf{m}_{12} + \mathbf{m}_{32} = [1,1]+[1,1] = [2,2]\] \[\mathbf{M}_3 = \mathbf{m}_{23} + \mathbf{m}_{03} = [1,1]+[2,1] = [3,2]\]
\[\text{agg} = \begin{bmatrix} 2&4 \\\\ 3&2 \\\\ 2&2 \\\\ 3&2 \end{bmatrix}\]




Look at rows 1 and 3:
\[\mathbf{M}_1 = \mathbf{M}_3 = [3,2]\]
After one full round of message passing, nodes 1 and 3 have identical aggregated summaries. Any GNN update applied to these identical \(\mathbf{M}\) values will produce identical updated features — the network provably cannot distinguish them.
This isn’t a weight-tuning problem. No training fixes it. It’s a structural limitation — the neighborhood of node 1 and node 3 look identical from the graph’s perspective.

objectID: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-4---node-update
href: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-4---node-update
title: GNN Message Passing
text:
Before the math — think about what \(\mathbf{M}_i\) alone contains.
\(\mathbf{M}_0 = [2,4]\) summarizes node 0’s neighborhood. But it contains no information about node 0 itself. If you replaced \(\mathbf{h}_0\) with \(\mathbf{M}_0\) directly, node 0 would forget it ever had feature \(h_0 = 2\).
The fix is to concatenate before updating:
\[\mathbf{h}_i' = \text{MLP}_\text{update}([\mathbf{h}_i \| \mathbf{M}_i])\]
The node retains its own identity alongside its neighborhood summary. The update MLP then learns how to blend the two.




For node 0, using the same identity weights and \(H=2\), but now \(d_{in} = 3\) because \([\mathbf{h}_0 \| \mathbf{M}_0] = [2 \| 2, 4] = [2, 2, 4]^\top\):
\[\mathbf{W}_\text{update} = \begin{bmatrix} 1&0&0 \\\\ 0&1&0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix}0 \\\\ 0\end{bmatrix}\]
\[\mathbf{h}_0' = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0 \\\\ 0&1&0\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 2 \\\\ 4\end{bmatrix}\right) = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 2\end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 2\end{bmatrix}\]




Applying the same procedure to every node:










Node
\(\mathbf{h}_i\)
\(\mathbf{M}_i\)
\([\mathbf{h}_i \Vert \mathbf{M}_i]\)
\(\mathbf{h}_i'\)




0
\([2]\)
\([2,4]\)
\([2,2,4]\)
\([2,2]\)


1
\([1]\)
\([3,2]\)
\([1,3,2]\)
\([1,3]\)


2
\([1]\)
\([2,2]\)
\([1,2,2]\)
\([1,2]\)


3
\([1]\)
\([3,2]\)
\([1,3,2]\)
\([1,3]\)



Notice nodes 1 and 3 produce identical \(\mathbf{h}'\) — the WL collapse persists through the update, exactly as predicted.

Question: Node 2 received from nodes 1 and 3, both with \(h=1\). Why is \(\mathbf{h}_2'\) different from \(\mathbf{h}_1'\) despite all three nodes starting with \(h=1\)?
Because, node 2’s neighbors both have \(h=1\), while node 1’s neighbors have \(h=2\) and \(h=1\)

On \(\mathbf{W}_\text{update}\)
\(\mathbf{W}_\text{update}\) — was assummed to be such and chosen deliberately to keep the arithmetic transparent. In a real GNN, \(\mathbf{W}_\text{update}\) is learned during training via backpropagation, just like CNN filter weights.
The \([2 \times 3]\) shape comes directly from the dimensions of the input and output:
\[\mathbf{W}_\text{update} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{out} \times d_{in}}\]
Here:

\(d_{in} = 3\) because \([\mathbf{h}_i \| \mathbf{M}_i]\) concatenates a scalar \(\mathbf{h}_i \in \mathbb{R}^1\) with \(\mathbf{M}_i \in \mathbb{R}^2\), giving a 3-dimensional input
\(d_{out} = 2\) because we chose hidden dimension \(H = 2\)

So the matrix must be \([2 \times 3]\) to satisfy the matrix multiplication \(\mathbf{W}_\text{update} \cdot [\mathbf{h}_i \| \mathbf{M}_i]\):
\[[2 \times 3] \cdot [3 \times 1] = [2 \times 1]\]
The general rule — consistent with everything you’ve seen so far:
\[\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{out} \times d_{in}}, \quad d_{in} = F + H, \quad d_{out} = H\]

objectID: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-5---full-data-flow
href: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-5---full-data-flow
title: GNN Message Passing
text:
Every transformation in one pipeline, using \(C_4\) values throughout:
\[[N, F] \xrightarrow{\text{gather}} [2E,\ 2F] \xrightarrow{\text{MLP}_\text{msg}} [2E, H] \xrightarrow{\text{scatter\_add}} [N, H] \xrightarrow{\text{MLP}_\text{update}} [N, H]\]
Substituting \(N=4,\ F=1,\ E=4,\ H=2\):
\[[4,1] \xrightarrow{\text{gather}} [8,2] \xrightarrow{\text{MLP}_\text{msg}} [8,2] \xrightarrow{\text{scatter\_add}} [4,2] \xrightarrow{\text{MLP}_\text{update}} [4,2]\]
Each arrow is one operation. Let’s nail what happens at each boundary.

Step 1 — Gather: \([4,1] \to [8,2]\)
For every directed edge, look up the features of its source and destination node from \(\mathbf{H}\), then concatenate them:
\[\text{edge } k: \quad [\mathbf{h}_{\text{src}[k]} \| \mathbf{h}_{\text{dst}[k]}]\]
8 edges × 2 concatenated scalars = \([8 \times 2]\). No arithmetic yet — pure lookup and concatenation.

Step 2 — \(\text{MLP}_\text{msg}\): \([8,2] \to [8,2]\)
Each of the 8 rows passes independently through the same MLP. Shape stays \([8 \times 2]\) because \(d_{in} = 2F = 2\) and \(d_{out} = H = 2\).

Step 3 — scatter_add_: \([8,2] \to [4,2]\)
8 edge-indexed messages collapse into 4 node-indexed aggregations. Edge dimension \(\to\) node dimension. This is the only step that reduces the number of rows.

Step 4 — \(\text{MLP}_\text{update}\): \([4,2] \to [4,2]\)
Each node concatenates its own feature \(\mathbf{h}_i \in \mathbb{R}^1\) with \(\mathbf{M}_i \in \mathbb{R}^2\) internally, giving \(d_{in} = 3\), \(d_{out} = H = 2\). The 4 nodes pass independently through the same MLP. Output is the new feature matrix \(\mathbf{H}' \in [4 \times 2]\).




Before message passing, every node knew only itself:
\[\mathbf{h}_0 = 2, \quad \mathbf{h}_1 = \mathbf{h}_2 = \mathbf{h}_3 = 1\]
After one round, every node knows itself plus a summary of its immediate neighbors:
\[\mathbf{h}_0' = [2,2], \quad \mathbf{h}_1' = \mathbf{h}_3' = [1,3], \quad \mathbf{h}_2' = [1,2]\]
Each updated feature vector encodes a 1-hop neighborhood summary. Stack another round on top, and every node would encode a 2-hop summary. This is how GNNs build up global graph awareness — one hop at a time.

Question: The table shows node 0 on \(C_4\) sees nodes 1 and 3 after layer 1, then node 2 after layer 2. Why does it take two layers to reach node 2, not one?
Receptive field grows by exactly one hop per layer.
Node 0’s neighbors are \(\{1, 3\}\) — one hop away. Node 2 is two hops away via \(0\to1\to2\) or \(0\to3\to2\). One layer reaches one hop. Two layers reach two hops. This is purely graph distance.
This is called the receptive field — directly analogous to CNN receptive field, where stacking convolutional layers expands the spatial region each output pixel “sees.”
Question: The portion says mean pooling is preferred over sum because it’s invariant to graph size. Can you think of a scenario where sum pooling would actually lose information that mean pooling preserves, or vice versa?

Graph A: 3 nodes, all with feature value 1 \(\to\) sum = 3; mean = 1
Graph B: 6 nodes, all with feature value 1 \(\to\) sum = 6; mean = 1

If energy scales with atom count, sum is the natural choice — bigger molecule, larger sum, larger prediction. Mean would wash that out.
QM9 uses mean specifically because the model handles variable-size molecules and needs stable training — scale invariance is prioritized over size-sensitivity. Neither is universally correct — it’s a design choice driven by the task.

The new concepts to focus are:







Concept
Core idea




Receptive field
depth = hops seen


Global pooling
nodes → single graph vector


Permutation invariance
addition commutes, so node ordering doesn’t matter

objectID: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-6---input-projection
href: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-6---input-projection
title: GNN Message Passing
text:
You have raw node features \(\mathbf{X}\) going into the network. On QM9, each atom carries 11 chemically meaningful features — atom type, charge, etc. On \(C_4\), each node carries a scalar, so \(F=1\).
The problem: every MPNN layer operates in a uniform hidden space of dimension \(H=64\). You cannot feed a 1-dimensional or 11-dimensional vector directly into a layer expecting 64-dimensional inputs.
The fix is a single linear projection — one MLP layer with no hidden depth:
\[\mathbf{H}^{(0)} = \text{ReLU}(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}_\text{in})\]



Symbol
Shape
Meaning




\(\mathbf{X}\)
\([N \times F]\)
raw features


\(\mathbf{W}_\text{in}\)
\([F \times H]\)
learned projection


\(\mathbf{H}^{(0)}\)
\([N \times H]\)
projected features



On \(C_4\) with \(F=1\), \(H=2\) for simplicity:
\[\mathbf{W}_\text{in} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\]
\[\mathbf{H}^{(0)} = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1&1\end{bmatrix}\right) = \text{ReLU}\left(\begin{bmatrix}2&2 \\\\ 1&1 \\\\ 1&1 \\\\ 1&1\end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix}2&2 \\\\ 1&1 \\\\ 1&1 \\\\ 1&1\end{bmatrix}\]
Every node is now living in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) — same space, ready for message passing.

Correct. The matrix multiplication requires:
\[[N \times F] \cdot [F \times H] = [N \times H]\]
so \(\mathbf{W}_\text{in}\) must be \([F \times H] = [11 \times 64]\) — inner dimensions must match.

objectID: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-7---stacked-message-passing-receptive-field
href: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-7---stacked-message-passing-receptive-field
title: GNN Message Passing
text:
Every node now lives in a uniform \(H\)-dimensional space. A single MPNN layer — everything you built in Phases 2–4 — expands each node’s awareness by exactly one hop. Stacking \(L\) layers expands it by \(L\) hops.
Formally, each layer takes the current hidden state and produces the next:
\[\mathbf{H}^{(l+1)} = \text{MPNNLayer}\left(\mathbf{H}^{(l)},\ \mathcal{E}\right)\]
The superscript tracks depth, not time. \(\mathbf{H}^{(0)}\) is the projection output. \(\mathbf{H}^{(1)}\) encodes 1-hop neighborhoods. \(\mathbf{H}^{(2)}\) encodes 2-hop neighborhoods. And so on.




Tracing node 0 specifically across \(L=3\) layers:
Layer 0 → 1: Node 0 aggregates from \(\{1, 3\}\). Its new state \(\mathbf{h}_0^{(1)}\) encodes itself plus its direct neighbors.
Layer 1 → 2: Now \(\mathbf{h}_1^{(1)}\) already encodes node 1’s neighborhood \(\{0,2\}\). When node 0 aggregates from node 1 at layer 2, it indirectly pulls in node 2’s information. Node 0 has now seen 2 hops.
Layer 2 → 3: \(\mathbf{h}_2^{(2)}\) already encodes node 2’s neighborhood \(\{1,3\}\), which itself encoded \(\{0,2\}\). Node 0 now sees the full graph.
The receptive field table makes this concrete:



Layer
Node 0 sees




0
\(\{0\}\)


1
\(\{0,1,3\}\)


2
\(\{0,1,2,3\}\)


3
full graph



\(C_4\) has diameter 2 — any node reaches any other in at most 2 hops — so \(L=2\) already saturates the receptive field here. Layer 3 adds no new nodes but re-encodes richer higher-order structure.




This is exactly your CNN receptive field intuition:



CNN
GNN




Stacking conv layers
Stacking MPNN layers


Spatial neighborhood grows
Graph neighborhood grows


Kernel size controls hop width
Aggregation controls hop width


Deep = sees more pixels
Deep = sees more nodes



The difference: CNN neighborhoods are regular grids. GNN neighborhoods are irregular graphs — each node can have a different number of neighbors.

Question: On a path graph \(0\leftrightarrow1\leftrightarrow2\leftrightarrow3\leftrightarrow4\) with \(L=2\) layers, which nodes can node 0 see?
The node 0 can see: \(\{0,1,2\}\).

Correct — two hops from node 0 on a path graph reaches nodes \(\{0,1,2\}\). Node 3 is 3 hops away, node 4 is 4 hops — both out of reach with \(L=2\).

objectID: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-8---global-mean-pooling
href: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-8---global-mean-pooling
title: GNN Message Passing
text:
After \(L\) layers, you have \(\mathbf{H}^{(L)} \in [N \times H]\) — one rich vector per node. But the task is graph-level regression: predict a single number \(U_0\) for the entire molecule.
You need to compress \(N\) node vectors into one graph vector. This is pooling — same concept as spatial pooling in CNN, but over graph nodes instead of pixel grids.




\[\mathbf{g} = \frac{1}{4}\left(\mathbf{h}_0^{(L)} + \mathbf{h}_1^{(L)} + \mathbf{h}_2^{(L)} + \mathbf{h}_3^{(L)}\right)\]
Using our \(H=2\) updated states from Phase 4:
\[\mathbf{g} = \frac{1}{4}\left(\begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 2\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}1 \\\\3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}1 \\\\ 2\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}1 \\\\ 3\end{bmatrix}\right) = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix}5 \\\\ 10\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1.25 \\\\ 2.5\end{bmatrix}\]
One vector summarizing the entire graph.




In practice, a training batch contains multiple graphs packed together. Nodes from different graphs sit in the same matrix but must pool separately.
The batch tensor handles this — it maps every node to its graph index:
\[\texttt{batch} = [0,0,0,1,1]\]
This says nodes \(\{0,1,2\}\) belong to graph 0, nodes \(\{3,4\}\) belong to graph 1. scatter_add_ then accumulates by graph index — exactly the same mechanic as Phase 0.4, but now the index is a graph label instead of a node label.
\[\mathbf{g}_0 = \frac{\mathbf{h}_0+\mathbf{h}_1+\mathbf{h}_2}{3}, \quad \mathbf{g}_1 = \frac{\mathbf{h}_3+\mathbf{h}_4}{2}\]
Two graphs, two pooled vectors, computed in one pass.

Question: If a batch contains three graphs with 4, 2, and 6 nodes respectively, what does batch look like and what shape is the pooled output?
One entry per node, grouped by graph, one pooled vector per graph, i.e.:
batch \(= [0,0,0,0,1,1,2,2,2,2,2,2]\), output \([3 \times H]\)

objectID: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-9---readout-mlp
href: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-9---readout-mlp
title: GNN Message Passing
text:
You now have a single graph vector \(\mathbf{g} \in \mathbb{R}^H\) summarizing the entire molecule.
You have \(\mathbf{g} \in \mathbb{R}^{64}\) — a rich graph-level summary. The final step compresses it to a single scalar \(\hat{y}\):
\[\hat{y} = \mathbf{W}_2\,\text{ReLU}(\mathbf{W}_1\mathbf{g} + \mathbf{b}_1) + \mathbf{b}_2\]
Two MLP layers — nothing new mathematically. Just the same affine-then-nonlinearity chain from Phase 0.3, applied twice.
Tracing shapes with \(H=2\) and an intermediate dimension of 2:








Step
Operation
Shape




Input
\(\mathbf{g}\)
\([2]\)


Layer 1
\(\mathbf{W}_1\mathbf{g}+\mathbf{b}_1\)
\([2\times2]\cdot[2]=[2]\)


ReLU
elementwise
\([2]\)


Layer 2
\(\mathbf{W}_2(\cdot)+\mathbf{b}_2\)
\([1\times2]\cdot[2]=[1]\)


Output
\(\hat{y}\)
scalar







This is the final architectural guarantee worth understanding precisely.
Suppose you relabel \(C_4\)’s nodes — swap node 0 and node 2. Does \(\hat{y}\) change?
It doesn’t — and here’s exactly why, tracing back through the pipeline:

Mean pooling sums node vectors and divides. Addition commutes — order of summation is irrelevant. \(\mathbf{g}\) is identical regardless of node ordering.
MPNN layers aggregate via scatter_add_ — also a sum, also commutative.
Readout MLP is deterministic given \(\mathbf{g}\) — same input, same output.

The invariance is structural, not learned. No amount of training could break it — it is baked into the choice of sum/mean as the aggregation operator.
This matters physically: a molecule’s energy \(U_0\) is a property of its atomic structure, not of how you number its atoms.

objectID: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-10---full-pipeline-on-c_4
href: catw/GNN Message Passing/gnn_message_passing.html#phase-10---full-pipeline-on-c_4
title: GNN Message Passing
text:
Putting everything together in one trace:
\[\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix}2 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1 \\\\ 1\end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{X}\ [4\times1]} \xrightarrow{\mathbf{W}_\text{in}} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix}2&2 \\\\ 1&1 \\\\ 1&1 \\\\ 1&1\end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{H}^{(0)}\ [4\times2]} \xrightarrow{L\times\text{MPNN}} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix}2&2 \\\\ 1&3 \\\\ 1&2 \\\\ 1&3\end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{H}^{(L)}\ [4\times2]} \xrightarrow{\text{mean}} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix}1.25 \\\\ 2.5\end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{g}\ [2]} \xrightarrow{\text{MLP}} \underbrace{\hat{y}}_{\text{scalar}}\]
Every shape transition follows one rule: node-indexed \([N\times H]\) or edge-indexed \([2E\times H]\), collapsing to a single vector at pooling.

objectID: catw/Session 2/index.html
href: catw/Session 2/index.html
title: Session 2
text:
Ground Metric Defines the fundamental “ruler” or cost function used to measure the distance between two individual points in a space before any global transport plan is calculated. It determines how the geometry of the underlying space is perceived, such as using Euclidean distance for physical coordinates or cosine similarity for semantic embeddings. The choice of this metric is critical because it dictates the “effort” required to move mass in the broader optimal transport problem.
Inner-level vs Outer-level Describes a hierarchical structure where the inner-level measures differences between small components, such as words in a sentence, while the outer-level uses those results to compare the larger objects, such as entire documents. This separation allows the model to capture fine-grained local details while maintaining a global perspective on the relationship between complex entities. It is essential for processing data that exists at multiple scales or levels of abstraction.
Nested Wasserstein Distance Applies a Wasserstein calculation inside another to handle structured data where elements are themselves probability distributions, such as high-resolution image patches. This recursive approach ensures that the distance between large objects respects the internal geometry and mass distribution of their constituent parts. It provides a principled way to measure similarity in complex datasets that simpler, single-layer metrics cannot capture.
Generative Model Training Teaches an artificial intelligence to produce new, original data samples that share the statistical and geometric characteristics of a training dataset. By minimizing a divergence measure—often a Wasserstein-based loss—the model learns to map simple noise into complex distributions like realistic images or coherent text. This process focuses on capturing the underlying manifold of the data to ensure high-fidelity and diverse outputs.
Pixel-wise L2 Metric Computes the straight-line Euclidean distance between images by comparing the intensity of each corresponding pixel one by one. While computationally simple, it is often criticized for being “blind” to spatial structure, as a slight shift in an object can result in a massive distance value despite the image looking nearly identical to a human. This limitation is a primary motivator for using optimal transport, which accounts for the movement of shapes rather than just static pixel values.
Class Coherent Matches Ensures that during the alignment of two different datasets, data points belonging to a specific category are mapped exclusively to the same category in the target domain. This constraint prevents “semantic drift,” where the model might incorrectly associate features of one class with those of another. Maintaining this coherence is vital for ensuring that knowledge transferred between domains remains accurate and logically sound.
Group-Norm Penalties Impose a mathematical “fine” during optimization to encourage the model to treat related data points as a single cohesive unit rather than independent entities. These penalties force the algorithm to select or transform features in a way that respects group structures, such as all pixels within a specific object. They are frequently used in multi-task learning and domain adaptation to ensure that the learned representations are structurally consistent.
Submodular Cost Functions Define a mathematical rule for calculating transport costs where the marginal “price” of moving additional mass decreases as more is moved, reflecting a principle of diminishing returns. This type of cost function is used to model complex, non-linear relationships and interactions between elements in a set. Incorporating submodularity allows optimal transport to better represent real-world scenarios where costs are not simply additive.
Unsupervised Domain Adaptation Addresses the challenge of taking a model trained on fully labeled data and successfully applying it to a new, related dataset that contains no labels at all. It typically involves finding a shared feature space where the distributions of the source and target data overlap, allowing the model’s predictions to remain valid. This is a core problem in machine learning, as real-world data is often abundant but lacks expensive ground-truth annotations.
Proper Distances Refer to measurements that satisfy strict mathematical axioms, most notably the triangle inequality, which ensures that the distance between two points is always less than or equal to the sum of distances through a third point. Adhering to these rules guarantees that the distance metric is consistent, reliable, and behaves predictably within a geometric space. In computational optimal transport, using proper distances like the Wasserstein metric allows for stable and theoretically sound comparisons between distributions.
Static Pair of Tasks Refers to a research framework that evaluates a model using a fixed, singular source and target domain, such as adapting from photographs to sketches. While this allows for deep analysis of specific shifts, it may fail to demonstrate how a method generalizes across a wider variety of data distributions. Moving beyond static pairs is necessary to ensure that geometric deep learning techniques are robust against diverse real-world scenarios.
Probability Simplex Defines the mathematical space containing all possible probability distributions for a discrete set of outcomes, where every point represents a vector of non-negative values that sum to exactly one. It forms a geometric shape, like a triangle in 2D or a tetrahedron in 3D, representing the constraints of probability theory. This space is the primary “playground” for optimal transport, as it contains the distributions we seek to move and compare.
Scales Cubically Describes a specific increase in computational complexity where doubling the size of the input data results in an eightfold increase in processing time. This relationship, expressed as \(O(n^3)\), often makes exact optimal transport calculations prohibitively expensive for large datasets. Identifying algorithms that avoid this cubic growth is a central goal in modern computational optimal transport.
Entropy Regularization Adds a smoothing term to the optimal transport objective function to encourage a more “spread out” transport plan rather than a sparse, one-to-one mapping. This modification transforms a difficult optimization problem into a strictly convex one that can be solved rapidly using the Sinkhorn algorithm. While it introduces a slight approximation error, the gain in computational speed makes it a standard tool for large-scale deep learning.
Relative Entropy (Kullback-Leibler Divergence) Measures how much information is lost when one probability distribution is used to approximate a second, reference distribution. Unlike the Wasserstein distance, it does not account for the underlying geometry of the space and is sensitive only to the overlap in probability mass. It is a fundamental tool in information geometry for quantifying the divergence between statistical models.
Joint Distribution Describes the probability of multiple random variables occurring simultaneously, capturing the statistical dependencies between them. In optimal transport, a transport plan is mathematically viewed as a joint distribution where the “marginal” distributions are the source and target datasets. Understanding this joint structure is key to identifying how mass from one distribution is assigned to another.
Conditional Probability Calculates the likelihood of an event occurring given that another specific event has already taken place. It allows models to update their predictions based on new evidence or context, such as predicting a label given a specific set of input features. This concept is essential for defining how features and labels relate within a manifold structure.
Product Space Forms a new mathematical environment by combining two or more existing spaces, where each point in the new space is an ordered pair or tuple from the originals. This construction allows optimal transport to move mass from one coordinate system to another by operating on the combined space of all possible origins and destinations. It is the geometric stage upon which coupling and transport plans are defined.
Statistical Divergence Acts as a mathematical function to quantify the difference between two probability distributions, serving as a generalized notion of distance. It encompasses a wide range of measures, including KL divergence and the Wasserstein distance, each with different sensitivities to the shape and overlap of data. Choosing the right divergence is critical for effectively training generative models and aligning disparate data domains.
Mapping Establishes a systematic rule that assigns every element from a source set to a specific element in a target set. In the context of geometric deep learning, a mapping might describe how a high-dimensional image is transformed into a low-dimensional vector or how one data manifold is projected onto another. The goal is often to find a mapping that preserves the essential geometric or semantic properties of the original data.
Representational Fidelity Ensures that a data representation captures the full complexity and diversity of a distribution rather than collapsing it into a single summary statistic or average point. It maintains the nuanced “spread” of information, such as the variety within a category, which is essential for accurate modeling of intricate data manifolds. High fidelity prevents the loss of critical structural details that simpler approximations might ignore.
Lifting the Metric Extends a distance formula designed for individual points to a higher-level framework capable of measuring the discrepancy between entire sets or distributions of points. This mathematical elevation allows global comparisons between complex objects by aggregating local geometric relationships into a single coherent distance. It is the core mechanism that enables optimal transport to use point-to-point costs to derive distribution-to-distribution distances.
Non-parametric Describes a methodology that makes no prior assumptions about the specific mathematical shape or distribution of the underlying data. Instead of forcing data into a predefined model, such as a bell curve, it allows the complexity of the model to grow or adapt based on the observed samples. This flexibility is vital in geometric deep learning for capturing irregular or high-dimensional structures that don’t fit standard statistical templates.
Uniform Empirical Measure Assigns an equal probability weight to every discrete observation in a dataset, treating each sample as equally significant. It serves as a discrete approximation of a continuous distribution, allowing mathematical operations to be performed on finite sets of data. In optimal transport, this is the standard way to represent a raw dataset before computing the cost of moving its mass.
Covariance Matrix Quantifies the relationships between different dimensions of a random vector to describe the overall shape and orientation of a data cloud. The diagonal elements represent the variance of individual features, while the off-diagonal elements indicate how features change together. It acts as a geometric summary of the spread of the data, which is essential for defining Mahalanobis distances or Gaussian-based transport plans.
Gaussian Distribution Represents a probability distribution that is symmetric about the mean, famously characterized by its bell-shaped curve. It is defined entirely by its mean and covariance, making it a highly efficient model for summarizing many real-world phenomena. In the context of optimal transport, comparing two Gaussians leads to specific closed-form solutions that bypass the need for expensive iterative solvers.
Analytic Form Provides a direct “plug and play” formula that yields a result through a fixed sequence of operations without requiring iterative approximation. Because it does not rely on the model “guessing” or refining its answer through training loops, it is significantly faster and more numerically stable. This efficiency is critical for embedding optimal transport distances directly into the loss functions of deep neural networks.
Matrix Square Root Identifies a matrix that, when multiplied by itself, produces the original covariance matrix, effectively representing a multidimensional version of standard deviation. It is used to transform or “whiten” data so that its variance becomes uniform across all directions. This operation is a key component in the Bures-Wasserstein distance, as it accounts for how the spread of one distribution must be reshaped to match another.
Trace (tr) Calculates the sum of the diagonal elements of a square matrix to provide a single-number summary of the total variance or “spread” across all dimensions. In geometric calculations, it often represents the total energy or magnitude of a transformation. It is a fundamental operation in the formula for the Wasserstein distance between Gaussians, where it helps quantify the difference in the scale of the distributions.
Bures Metric Measures the distance between two covariance matrices or quantum states to determine how their statistical spreads differ. In optimal transport, it forms the “variance” portion of the Wasserstein distance when comparing Gaussian distributions. This metric is essential for understanding how the orientation and scaling of data clouds shift between different domains or tasks.
Commuting Matrices Describe two matrices \(A\) and \(B\) that satisfy the property \(AB = BA\), indicating that the linear transformations they represent can be applied in any order without changing the result. In data science, this often implies that the datasets share the same principal axes or eigenvectors, meaning their features vary along the same underlying directions. This alignment simplifies the calculation of distances between distributions by allowing simultaneous diagonalization.
Frobenius Norm Measures the overall magnitude or “size” of a matrix by taking the square root of the sum of the squares of all its individual elements. It acts as a generalization of the Euclidean distance for matrices, providing a single scalar value that captures total intensity. This norm is frequently used in optimization to penalize large changes in model weights or to measure the distance between two different linear transformations.
2-Wasserstein Distance Defines a specific version of optimal transport distance that uses the squared Euclidean distance as the ground cost for moving mass. This particular formulation heavily penalizes moving mass over long distances, which mathematically results in a unique, smoother transport plan compared to other versions. It is highly valued in geometric deep learning because it allows for the definition of “averages” between distributions, known as Fréchet means or barycenters.
Algebraic Convenience Refers to the practical advantage of using mathematical models that result in simple, direct formulas that a computer can evaluate instantly. These solutions avoid the need for expensive, iterative simulation or complex optimization loops that consume significant time and memory. Prioritizing algebraic convenience allows researchers to scale advanced optimal transport techniques to massive datasets or real-time applications.
Gelbrich Distance Provides a mathematical lower bound for the Wasserstein distance between any two distributions by comparing only their respective means and covariance matrices. It essentially treats any data cloud as if it were a Gaussian to provide a quick, principled estimate of the distance between them. This serves as a useful diagnostic tool for assessing distribution shifts when the full, non-parametric transport plan is too difficult to compute.
Embed Transforms a high-dimensional or complex object, such as a dataset, image, or abstract concept, into a simpler mathematical structure like a single point or an ellipse within a vector space. This process preserves the essential relationships between objects so that semantic similarity can be measured as geometric distance. Embedding is the foundational step that allows neural networks to “reason” about disparate types of information in a unified way.
Elliptic Distributions Encompass a broad family of probability distributions, including the Gaussian, whose constant-density contours form ellipses or higher-dimensional ovals. While they share the symmetrical structure of a bell curve, they can accommodate heavier “tails” or sharper peaks to better fit real-world data that contains frequent outliers. This family is useful in optimal transport because many of the efficient closed-form solutions found for Gaussians also apply to these more flexible shapes.
Discrete Measure Represents a dataset as a collection of individual, separate points—commonly called a point cloud—where each point carries a specific amount of “weight” or probability mass. Unlike a continuous curve, which covers every possible value in a range, this measure focuses only on the specific observations actually present in the data. This is the most common format for real-world data in computational optimal transport, as it matches the way information is stored in digital arrays.
Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) Represent complex, multi-clumped data by combining multiple individual bell curves, each with its own mean and variance. This allow the model to approximate highly irregular distributions by treating them as a sum of simpler, well-understood parts. They are widely used in geometric learning to cluster data or to provide a flexible but mathematically tractable description of a dataset’s density.
Admissible Transportation Couplings Define the complete set of valid “plans” for redistributing mass from a source distribution to a target distribution while respecting the total amount of data available in each. Every plan in this set ensures that the amount of mass moved out of a source point and into a target point sums correctly to the original totals. The goal of optimal transport is to search through this entire set to find the single coupling that minimizes the total movement cost.
Gromov-Wasserstein Distance Measures the similarity between two datasets by comparing the internal distances between points within each set rather than matching their absolute coordinates. This allows for the alignment of distributions even when they exist in different dimensionalities or entirely different coordinate systems, such as matching a 3D shape to a 2D image. It serves as a foundational tool for relational learning and cross-domain alignment where a direct point-to-point mapping is physically impossible.
Leveraging Employs a specific attribute, tool, or existing data structure as a strategic advantage to achieve a more complex computational or analytical result. In geometric deep learning, this often involves using the intrinsic symmetries of a manifold or the pre-trained weights of a model to simplify a new optimization problem. It describes the practical application of available resources to enhance the performance or efficiency of a target algorithm.

objectID: catw/Session 2/index.html#concepts-from-research-paper-geometric-dataset-distances-via-optimal-transport
href: catw/Session 2/index.html#concepts-from-research-paper-geometric-dataset-distances-via-optimal-transport
title: Session 2
text:
Ground Metric Defines the fundamental “ruler” or cost function used to measure the distance between two individual points in a space before any global transport plan is calculated. It determines how the geometry of the underlying space is perceived, such as using Euclidean distance for physical coordinates or cosine similarity for semantic embeddings. The choice of this metric is critical because it dictates the “effort” required to move mass in the broader optimal transport problem.
Inner-level vs Outer-level Describes a hierarchical structure where the inner-level measures differences between small components, such as words in a sentence, while the outer-level uses those results to compare the larger objects, such as entire documents. This separation allows the model to capture fine-grained local details while maintaining a global perspective on the relationship between complex entities. It is essential for processing data that exists at multiple scales or levels of abstraction.
Nested Wasserstein Distance Applies a Wasserstein calculation inside another to handle structured data where elements are themselves probability distributions, such as high-resolution image patches. This recursive approach ensures that the distance between large objects respects the internal geometry and mass distribution of their constituent parts. It provides a principled way to measure similarity in complex datasets that simpler, single-layer metrics cannot capture.
Generative Model Training Teaches an artificial intelligence to produce new, original data samples that share the statistical and geometric characteristics of a training dataset. By minimizing a divergence measure—often a Wasserstein-based loss—the model learns to map simple noise into complex distributions like realistic images or coherent text. This process focuses on capturing the underlying manifold of the data to ensure high-fidelity and diverse outputs.
Pixel-wise L2 Metric Computes the straight-line Euclidean distance between images by comparing the intensity of each corresponding pixel one by one. While computationally simple, it is often criticized for being “blind” to spatial structure, as a slight shift in an object can result in a massive distance value despite the image looking nearly identical to a human. This limitation is a primary motivator for using optimal transport, which accounts for the movement of shapes rather than just static pixel values.
Class Coherent Matches Ensures that during the alignment of two different datasets, data points belonging to a specific category are mapped exclusively to the same category in the target domain. This constraint prevents “semantic drift,” where the model might incorrectly associate features of one class with those of another. Maintaining this coherence is vital for ensuring that knowledge transferred between domains remains accurate and logically sound.
Group-Norm Penalties Impose a mathematical “fine” during optimization to encourage the model to treat related data points as a single cohesive unit rather than independent entities. These penalties force the algorithm to select or transform features in a way that respects group structures, such as all pixels within a specific object. They are frequently used in multi-task learning and domain adaptation to ensure that the learned representations are structurally consistent.
Submodular Cost Functions Define a mathematical rule for calculating transport costs where the marginal “price” of moving additional mass decreases as more is moved, reflecting a principle of diminishing returns. This type of cost function is used to model complex, non-linear relationships and interactions between elements in a set. Incorporating submodularity allows optimal transport to better represent real-world scenarios where costs are not simply additive.
Unsupervised Domain Adaptation Addresses the challenge of taking a model trained on fully labeled data and successfully applying it to a new, related dataset that contains no labels at all. It typically involves finding a shared feature space where the distributions of the source and target data overlap, allowing the model’s predictions to remain valid. This is a core problem in machine learning, as real-world data is often abundant but lacks expensive ground-truth annotations.
Proper Distances Refer to measurements that satisfy strict mathematical axioms, most notably the triangle inequality, which ensures that the distance between two points is always less than or equal to the sum of distances through a third point. Adhering to these rules guarantees that the distance metric is consistent, reliable, and behaves predictably within a geometric space. In computational optimal transport, using proper distances like the Wasserstein metric allows for stable and theoretically sound comparisons between distributions.
Static Pair of Tasks Refers to a research framework that evaluates a model using a fixed, singular source and target domain, such as adapting from photographs to sketches. While this allows for deep analysis of specific shifts, it may fail to demonstrate how a method generalizes across a wider variety of data distributions. Moving beyond static pairs is necessary to ensure that geometric deep learning techniques are robust against diverse real-world scenarios.
Probability Simplex Defines the mathematical space containing all possible probability distributions for a discrete set of outcomes, where every point represents a vector of non-negative values that sum to exactly one. It forms a geometric shape, like a triangle in 2D or a tetrahedron in 3D, representing the constraints of probability theory. This space is the primary “playground” for optimal transport, as it contains the distributions we seek to move and compare.
Scales Cubically Describes a specific increase in computational complexity where doubling the size of the input data results in an eightfold increase in processing time. This relationship, expressed as \(O(n^3)\), often makes exact optimal transport calculations prohibitively expensive for large datasets. Identifying algorithms that avoid this cubic growth is a central goal in modern computational optimal transport.
Entropy Regularization Adds a smoothing term to the optimal transport objective function to encourage a more “spread out” transport plan rather than a sparse, one-to-one mapping. This modification transforms a difficult optimization problem into a strictly convex one that can be solved rapidly using the Sinkhorn algorithm. While it introduces a slight approximation error, the gain in computational speed makes it a standard tool for large-scale deep learning.
Relative Entropy (Kullback-Leibler Divergence) Measures how much information is lost when one probability distribution is used to approximate a second, reference distribution. Unlike the Wasserstein distance, it does not account for the underlying geometry of the space and is sensitive only to the overlap in probability mass. It is a fundamental tool in information geometry for quantifying the divergence between statistical models.
Joint Distribution Describes the probability of multiple random variables occurring simultaneously, capturing the statistical dependencies between them. In optimal transport, a transport plan is mathematically viewed as a joint distribution where the “marginal” distributions are the source and target datasets. Understanding this joint structure is key to identifying how mass from one distribution is assigned to another.
Conditional Probability Calculates the likelihood of an event occurring given that another specific event has already taken place. It allows models to update their predictions based on new evidence or context, such as predicting a label given a specific set of input features. This concept is essential for defining how features and labels relate within a manifold structure.
Product Space Forms a new mathematical environment by combining two or more existing spaces, where each point in the new space is an ordered pair or tuple from the originals. This construction allows optimal transport to move mass from one coordinate system to another by operating on the combined space of all possible origins and destinations. It is the geometric stage upon which coupling and transport plans are defined.
Statistical Divergence Acts as a mathematical function to quantify the difference between two probability distributions, serving as a generalized notion of distance. It encompasses a wide range of measures, including KL divergence and the Wasserstein distance, each with different sensitivities to the shape and overlap of data. Choosing the right divergence is critical for effectively training generative models and aligning disparate data domains.
Mapping Establishes a systematic rule that assigns every element from a source set to a specific element in a target set. In the context of geometric deep learning, a mapping might describe how a high-dimensional image is transformed into a low-dimensional vector or how one data manifold is projected onto another. The goal is often to find a mapping that preserves the essential geometric or semantic properties of the original data.
Representational Fidelity Ensures that a data representation captures the full complexity and diversity of a distribution rather than collapsing it into a single summary statistic or average point. It maintains the nuanced “spread” of information, such as the variety within a category, which is essential for accurate modeling of intricate data manifolds. High fidelity prevents the loss of critical structural details that simpler approximations might ignore.
Lifting the Metric Extends a distance formula designed for individual points to a higher-level framework capable of measuring the discrepancy between entire sets or distributions of points. This mathematical elevation allows global comparisons between complex objects by aggregating local geometric relationships into a single coherent distance. It is the core mechanism that enables optimal transport to use point-to-point costs to derive distribution-to-distribution distances.
Non-parametric Describes a methodology that makes no prior assumptions about the specific mathematical shape or distribution of the underlying data. Instead of forcing data into a predefined model, such as a bell curve, it allows the complexity of the model to grow or adapt based on the observed samples. This flexibility is vital in geometric deep learning for capturing irregular or high-dimensional structures that don’t fit standard statistical templates.
Uniform Empirical Measure Assigns an equal probability weight to every discrete observation in a dataset, treating each sample as equally significant. It serves as a discrete approximation of a continuous distribution, allowing mathematical operations to be performed on finite sets of data. In optimal transport, this is the standard way to represent a raw dataset before computing the cost of moving its mass.
Covariance Matrix Quantifies the relationships between different dimensions of a random vector to describe the overall shape and orientation of a data cloud. The diagonal elements represent the variance of individual features, while the off-diagonal elements indicate how features change together.
